The five selected items all use frequency-based ordinal scales (7–9 categories), making them scale-compatible and suitable for combining; p24 (binary radio habit) and p11_2 (online newspapers, H=0.41, near-zero variance) are excluded to avoid scale distortion and low-information items. PCA is chosen over a sum score because the items span slightly different scale lengths and the first component will capture the shared 'media engagement intensity' dimension while handling differential item variances.

Internet engagement and digital literacy is a multi-faceted construct spanning self-assessed skill, behavioral usage patterns, and mobile connectivity, requiring aggregation across items. p36 (self-assessed proficiency, H=1.64) anchors the literacy dimension, p47 (information-sharing frequency, H=1.72) captures active digital behavior, p39_7 (mobile data use) and p43_2 (work use) reflect practical engagement breadth, and p33_7 adds computer-based browsing; p45_2 (crime reporting, H=1.15) and p73 (event attendance, H=0.51) are excluded as they tap narrow civic behaviors with low entropy rather than general digital literacy. PCA is appropriate given the mix of ordinal frequency scales and binary-style items, allowing the first component to extract the dominant engagement-proficiency axis.

Media trust and quality evaluation is a coherent attitudinal construct best captured by combining evaluative judgments about content quality, coverage agreement, information reliability, and media sufficiency. The five selected items (p54, p55, p66, p69_1, p67) all use 4–7 category Likert-style ordinal scales measuring evaluative attitudes toward media, making them scale-compatible for averaging; p53_1 (trust in family members, 0–10) is excluded because it measures interpersonal rather than media institutional trust and uses an incompatible scale. A mean score is preferred over PCA given the conceptual homogeneity of the items and the desire for an interpretable composite that preserves the ordinal direction of trust/quality evaluation.

Perceived media social impact spans distinct sub-dimensions—gender portrayal, family influence, violence depiction, and regulatory attitudes—that together define the construct but are not interchangeable, favoring aggregation over a single item. The five selected items (p62_1, p59_1, p77_1, p76, p61) have the highest entropies in the cluster (H=1.68–1.95) and cover the core social impact facets; p60 (violence level assessment, H=1.20) and p63_1 (parental supervision importance, H=1.32) are excluded as lower-entropy items that tap narrower normative judgments. PCA is chosen because the items reflect heterogeneous but correlated social impact perceptions, and the first component will capture the shared variance of critical/concerned media attitudes while accommodating the differing scale lengths.

construct_clusters.name: ageism_and_negative_stereotypes
construct_clusters.description: Captures the degree to which respondents hold negative stereotypes or discriminatory attitudes toward older adults, including beliefs about their declining health, disengagement, and justification for age-based exclusion from employment.

construct_clusters.name: perceived_elder_abuse_and_mistreatment
construct_clusters.description: Measures respondents' awareness and beliefs about the nature, causes, and prevalence of abuse and mistreatment directed at older adults, including types of abuse recognized and personal knowledge of family-level abuse.

construct_clusters.name: state_and_family_responsibility_for_elder_care
construct_clusters.description: Taps into normative beliefs about who bears primary responsibility for the care of older adults — the state, family, or other actors — and the extent to which respondents endorse collective versus familial obligations.

construct_clusters.name: assessment_of_older_adult_wellbeing_and_social_conditions
construct_clusters.description: Reflects respondents' evaluations of the current and future quality of life, dignity, and social situation of older adults in Mexico, including perceived trends over time and the societal salience of their problems.

construct_clusters.name: support_for_elder_friendly_public_policy
construct_clusters.description: Measures the extent to which respondents endorse public investment and policy action on behalf of older adults, including willingness to allocate taxes for accessible infrastructure and the perceived importance of specific public policies.

sources.citation: North, M. S., & Fiske, S. T. (2012). An inconvenienced youth? Ageism and its potential intergenerational roots. Psychological Bulletin, 138(5), 982–997.
sources.relevance: Provides a comprehensive theoretical framework for understanding ageism as a multidimensional prejudice rooted in intergenerational tensions, directly relevant to measuring negative stereotypes toward older adults.
sources.key_finding: Ageism is driven by perceptions of resource succession, identity, and consumption, with younger cohorts endorsing prescriptive stereotypes that older adults should disengage from valued social roles.

sources.citation: Levy, B. R. (2009). Stereotype embodiment: A psychosocial approach to aging. Current Directions in Psychological Science, 18(6), 332–336.
sources.relevance: Demonstrates how negative age stereotypes are internalized and affect health outcomes, supporting the importance of measuring ageist attitudes at the population level.
sources.key_finding: Negative age stereotypes, when internalized, lead to worse functional health outcomes among older adults over time.

sources.citation: Montes de Oca, V. (2013). La discriminación hacia la vejez en la Ciudad de México: contrastes sociopolíticos y jurídicos a nivel nacional y local. Perspectivas Sociales, 15(1), 47–80.
sources.relevance: Directly examines age-based discrimination in Mexico City, providing context for measuring ageist attitudes in Mexican public opinion surveys.
sources.key_finding: Older adults in Mexico face systematic discrimination in employment, healthcare, and public spaces, reinforced by cultural stereotypes about declining productivity.

sources.citation: Palmore, E. B. (2001). The ageism survey: First findings. The Gerontologist, 41(5), 572–575.
sources.relevance: Introduces a validated instrument for measuring ageism experiences, providing methodological grounding for survey-based measurement of age-related stereotypes.
sources.key_finding: The majority of older adults reported experiencing multiple instances of ageism, with the most common being being told a joke that ridiculed older people and being patronized.

sources.citation: Bustillos, A., & Fernández-Ballesteros, R. (2013). Efecto de los estereotipos acerca de la vejez en la atención a adultos mayores. Salud Pública de México, 55(1), 104–105.
sources.relevance: Examines how ageist stereotypes in Spanish-speaking contexts affect healthcare provision to older adults, directly relevant to the Mexican context.
sources.key_finding: Healthcare professionals holding negative stereotypes about aging provided lower quality care to older adult patients.

causal_relationships.related_construct: labor market exclusion of older adults

sources.citation: Pillemer, K., Burnes, D., Riffin, C., & Lachs, M. S. (2016). Elder abuse: Global situation, risk factors, and prevention strategies. The Gerontologist, 56(S2), S194–S205.
sources.relevance: Provides a global review of elder abuse prevalence, risk factors, and measurement approaches, directly relevant to understanding public perceptions of elder mistreatment.
sources.key_finding: Approximately 10% of older adults experience some form of abuse, with psychological abuse and neglect being the most prevalent types globally.

sources.citation: Giraldo-Rodríguez, L., & Rosas-Carrasco, O. (2013). Development and psychometric properties of the Geriatric Mistreatment Scale. Geriatrics & Gerontology International, 13(2), 466–474.
sources.relevance: Develops and validates a mistreatment measurement scale specifically for the Mexican older adult population, providing direct methodological relevance.
sources.key_finding: The Geriatric Mistreatment Scale demonstrated strong psychometric properties and identified psychological abuse as the most common form of elder mistreatment in Mexico.

sources.citation: Ruelas-González, M. G., & Salgado-de Snyder, V. N. (2009). Factores asociados con el auto-reporte de maltrato en adultos mayores de México. Revista de Investigación Clínica, 61(5), 389–399.
sources.relevance: Examines self-reported mistreatment among older adults in Mexico and its associated sociodemographic factors.
sources.key_finding: Being female, having lower education, and living in poverty were significantly associated with higher self-reported elder mistreatment in Mexico.

sources.citation: World Health Organization. (2022). Abuse of older people. Fact sheet. Geneva: WHO.
sources.relevance: Provides an authoritative framework for defining and categorizing types of elder abuse, supporting the construct's multidimensional measurement approach.
sources.key_finding: Elder abuse affects 1 in 6 people aged 60 and older worldwide, yet only 1 in 24 cases is reported, highlighting the importance of measuring public awareness.

sources.citation: Saraceno, C., & Keck, W. (2010). Can we identify intergenerational policy regimes in Europe? European Societies, 12(5), 675–696.
sources.relevance: Provides a comparative framework for analyzing how different societies allocate care responsibilities between the state and families, applicable to understanding Mexican normative beliefs.
sources.key_finding: Countries vary systematically in how they distribute elder care responsibilities between state, market, and family, forming distinct intergenerational policy regimes.

sources.citation: Montes de Oca, V., & Hebrero, M. (2006). Eventos cruciales y ciclos familiares avanzados: el efecto del envejecimiento en los hogares de México. Papeles de Población, 12(50), 97–116.
sources.relevance: Examines the role of Mexican family structures in elder care provision and how demographic aging is transforming household-level care responsibilities.
sources.key_finding: Mexican families remain the primary source of elder care, but demographic transitions are straining traditional intergenerational support systems.

sources.citation: Robles-Silva, L. (2007). La invisibilidad del cuidado a los enfermos crónicos: Un estudio cualitativo en el barrio de Oblatos. Guadalajara: Editorial Universitaria.
sources.relevance: Provides qualitative evidence on the gendered and familial nature of chronic care for older adults in Mexico, contextualizing normative beliefs about care responsibility.
sources.key_finding: Caregiving for chronically ill older adults in Mexico falls disproportionately on female family members, often without institutional support or social recognition.

sources.citation: Huenchuan, S. (2013). Envejecimiento, solidaridad y protección social en América Latina y el Caribe: La hora de avanzar hacia la igualdad. Santiago: CEPAL.
sources.relevance: Analyzes the tension between state and family responsibility for elder care across Latin America, providing regional context for Mexican public opinion.
sources.key_finding: Latin American countries are transitioning from familistic care models toward mixed systems, but public expectations increasingly demand greater state involvement in elder protection.

causal_relationships.related_construct: political culture and trust in government

sources.citation: Fernández-Ballesteros, R. (2011). Quality of life in old age: Problematic issues. Applied Research in Quality of Life, 6(1), 21–40.
sources.relevance: Provides a theoretical and empirical framework for assessing quality of life in old age, including subjective and objective dimensions relevant to public perceptions.
sources.key_finding: Quality of life in old age is multidimensional, encompassing health, social participation, economic security, and subjective well-being, and varies significantly by cultural context.

sources.citation: González, K. D., & Ham-Chande, R. (2007). Funcionalidad y salud: una tipología del envejecimiento en México. Salud Pública de México, 49(Supl. 4), S448–S458.
sources.relevance: Directly assesses health and functional status of older adults in Mexico, providing empirical context for understanding public perceptions of elder wellbeing.
sources.key_finding: Mexican older adults show heterogeneous aging trajectories, with significant proportions experiencing functional limitations that compromise their quality of life.

sources.citation: Wong, R., Michaels-Obregón, A., & Palloni, A. (2017). Cohort profile: The Mexican Health and Aging Study (MHAS). International Journal of Epidemiology, 46(2), e2.
sources.relevance: Describes the major longitudinal study of aging in Mexico, providing the empirical foundation for understanding trends in older adult wellbeing.
sources.key_finding: MHAS data reveal declining health trajectories among Mexican older adults, with socioeconomic disparities widening over time.

sources.citation: CONEVAL. (2019). Pobreza y personas mayores en México. Ciudad de México: Consejo Nacional de Evaluación de la Política de Desarrollo Social.
sources.relevance: Provides official data on poverty and social conditions among older adults in Mexico, contextualizing public perceptions of elder wellbeing.
sources.key_finding: Approximately 41% of adults aged 65 and older in Mexico lived in poverty, with rural and indigenous older adults disproportionately affected.

sources.citation: Walker, A. (2009). The emergence and application of active aging in Europe. Journal of Aging & Social Policy, 21(1), 75–93.
sources.relevance: Examines the policy framework of active aging and public support for age-friendly policies in Europe, providing a comparative lens for Mexican policy attitudes.
sources.key_finding: Public support for active aging policies is shaped by welfare state traditions, demographic awareness, and intergenerational solidarity norms.

sources.citation: Huenchuan, S., & Rodríguez-Piñero, L. (2010). Envejecimiento y derechos humanos: Situación y perspectivas de protección. Santiago: CEPAL.
sources.relevance: Analyzes the rights-based framework for elder-friendly policies in Latin America, directly relevant to measuring Mexican public support for such policies.
sources.key_finding: Latin American countries have made significant normative advances in elder rights, but implementation gaps persist due to insufficient public investment and political will.

sources.citation: Garay Villegas, S., & Montes de Oca Zavala, V. (2011). La vejez en México: una mirada general sobre la situación socioeconómica y familiar de los hombres y mujeres adultos mayores. Perspectivas Sociales, 13(1), 143–165.
sources.relevance: Provides context on the socioeconomic conditions that drive demand for elder-friendly policies in Mexico.
sources.key_finding: Gender and socioeconomic inequalities among Mexican older adults underscore the need for targeted public policies addressing pension coverage, healthcare access, and social inclusion.

sources.citation: Rofman, R., Apella, I., & Vezza, E. (Eds.). (2015). Más allá de las pensiones contributivas: Catorce experiencias en América Latina. Buenos Aires: Banco Mundial.
sources.relevance: Reviews non-contributory pension programs across Latin America, providing evidence on public policy instruments that survey respondents may evaluate.
sources.key_finding: Non-contributory pensions have significantly reduced poverty among older adults in Latin America, with Mexico's programs showing measurable impacts on wellbeing.

causal_relationships.related_construct: assessment of older adult wellbeing and social conditions
beliefs about state vs. family responsibility
willingness to pay taxes / fiscal attitudes
support for social spending on children and youth

research_reviews.gap_justification: While there is growing literature on elder-friendly policy frameworks in Latin America, there is limited research specifically measuring Mexican public opinion on willingness to invest in elder-specific infrastructure and policy priorities. Most studies focus on policy design and outcomes rather than on citizen demand and attitudinal support for such policies. This construct fills a critical gap by capturing the demand side of the policy equation — understanding what the Mexican public is willing to support and prioritize. Given Mexico's rapid demographic aging and the expansion of programs like the Pensión para el Bienestar de las Personas Adultas Mayores, understanding public attitudes toward elder-friendly policy is essential for evaluating democratic legitimacy and sustainability of social spending. This construct also enables cross-domain analysis linking fiscal attitudes, trust in government, and intergenerational solidarity.

variable_strategies.justification: Ageism is inherently multi-dimensional, encompassing stereotypic beliefs, prejudicial attitudes, and discriminatory perceptions that no single item can fully capture. The four selected items (p13_1, p14_1, p26, p27) share a compatible 7-point scale and exhibit the highest entropy values (H=1.91–2.28), indicating good distributional spread and measurement information. Items p18_1 and p25_1 are excluded because their narrower scales (4 and 5 categories) and substantially lower entropy (H=0.78, 1.05) would distort a mean composite; mean-scoring the four 7-point items and binning to ≤5 categories preserves ordinal structure while reducing single-item measurement error.

Elder abuse perception is multi-faceted (awareness, prevalence beliefs, severity, type), requiring aggregation across items; however, the items have heterogeneous scales (4, 6, 4, 4 categories) and varying entropy, making a simple sum or mean inappropriate. PCA on the first component accommodates scale heterogeneity by standardizing inputs before extraction, capturing the dominant shared variance in perceived mistreatment. p20aa_1 is excluded because its 10-category structure and high entropy (H=2.64) suggest it may tap a distinct sub-dimension (e.g., specific abuse type frequency) that could distort the common factor; the remaining four items more coherently reflect general perceived abuse prevalence and seriousness.

Responsibility attribution for elder care spans both state and family dimensions and is genuinely multi-dimensional, requiring aggregation to capture the full construct. The items have moderately compatible but not identical scales (5–9 categories) and varying entropy (H=1.51–2.03), making PCA preferable to a raw sum or mean as it standardizes inputs and extracts the dominant attitudinal dimension. p44 is excluded due to its very low entropy (H=0.78, K=4), indicating a highly skewed distribution that would contribute little variance to the composite and could suppress the first principal component's interpretability.

Wellbeing and social conditions assessments are inherently multi-dimensional (economic, health, social integration, quality of life), requiring aggregation across items. p9, p10, p38, and p39 share a 6-point scale while p36 uses a 5-point scale—sufficiently compatible for mean-scoring after confirming directional alignment—and all have moderate-to-high entropy (H=1.65–2.27), ensuring informative spread. p37_1 (K=10) and p7_1a_1/p7_2a_1 (K=11, K=8) are excluded because their wider, potentially non-Likert response formats are scale-incompatible with the core 5–6 point items and risk introducing construct-irrelevant variance into the composite.

Support for elder-friendly public policy spans multiple policy domains (healthcare, housing, income support, social services), making aggregation essential to represent the construct's breadth. The items have heterogeneous scales (5, 5, 5, 6, and 13 categories), so PCA with standardized inputs is preferred over a raw mean to handle scale differences while extracting the dominant pro-policy attitude dimension. p40_1 (K=13, H=2.50) is retained because its high entropy provides valuable discriminating power, but PCA's standardization prevents its wider scale from dominating; p35 (H=1.35, the lowest) is excluded as its limited distributional spread contributes the least unique information to the latent policy-support factor.

construct_clusters.name: institutional_trust_justice
construct_clusters.description: Measures citizens' trust and confidence in formal justice institutions — including judges, prosecutor offices, the Supreme Court, and human rights commissions — to uphold rights and deliver justice. Captures perceived legitimacy and effectiveness of the justice system.

construct_clusters.name: authoritarian_security_tradeoff
construct_clusters.description: Measures the degree to which respondents accept rights violations or coercive state practices — such as torture, post-submission harm by police, and restricting human rights in the name of public security — reflecting a tolerance for authoritarianism when framed as necessary for safety.

construct_clusters.name: perceived_discrimination_social_exclusion
construct_clusters.description: Measures respondents' perceptions of and attitudes toward discriminatory treatment based on physical appearance, skin color, religion, disability, and sexual orientation, including both personal experiences of rights violations and observed societal discrimination against vulnerable groups.

construct_clusters.name: rights_awareness_personal_experience
construct_clusters.description: Measures respondents' direct personal experience with human rights violations and labor rights abuses, as well as their sense of protection against authority abuse, capturing the lived dimension of rights rather than abstract attitudes.

construct_clusters.name: social_rights_service_quality
construct_clusters.description: Measures citizens' evaluations of the state's fulfillment of social and economic rights — including public health services, public education quality, and dignified housing — reflecting perceived adequacy of social rights provision by the government.

sources.citation: Ríos Figueroa, J. (2007). Fragmentation of power and the emergence of an effective judiciary in Mexico, 1994–2002. Latin American Politics and Society, 49(1), 31–57.
sources.relevance: Examines the development of judicial independence and public perceptions of the Mexican Supreme Court's legitimacy, directly relevant to trust in judges and high courts.
sources.key_finding: Fragmentation of political power contributed to greater judicial independence, which in turn shaped public confidence in the judiciary's capacity to protect rights.

sources.citation: Magaloni, B. (2003). Authoritarianism, democracy, and the Supreme Court: Horizontal exchange and the rule of law in Mexico. In S. Mainwaring & C. Welna (Eds.), Democratic accountability in Latin America (pp. 266–305). Oxford University Press.
sources.relevance: Analyzes how citizens evaluate the Supreme Court's role in defending constitutional rights under democratic transition, directly mapping onto p21|DER.
sources.key_finding: Public trust in the Supreme Court increased as it began to exercise judicial review more actively, but remained conditioned by partisan perceptions.

sources.citation: Ingram, M. C. (2016). Crafting courts in new democracies: The politics of subnational judicial reform in Brazil and Mexico. Cambridge University Press.
sources.relevance: Discusses variation in trust in justice institutions across Mexican states, including prosecutor offices and courts, relevant to p18–p20.
sources.key_finding: Institutional design choices at the subnational level significantly affect citizens' trust in courts and prosecution services.

sources.citation: Monsiváis-Carrillo, A. (2019). Institutional trust in Latin America: Measurement and conceptual issues. Política y Gobierno, 26(2), 161–184.
sources.relevance: Provides a conceptual and measurement framework for institutional trust in Latin American democracies, including justice institutions and human rights bodies.
sources.key_finding: Institutional trust in Latin America is multidimensional, with trust in justice institutions forming a distinct factor from trust in elected officials.

sources.citation: Saltalamacchia Ziccardi, N., & Covarrubias Velasco, A. (2011). La dimensión internacional de la reforma de derechos humanos: antecedentes históricos. In M. Carbonell & P. Salazar (Eds.), La reforma constitucional de derechos humanos: Un nuevo paradigma. UNAM.
sources.relevance: Contextualizes the role of human rights commissions (CNDH) in Mexico's institutional architecture, directly relevant to p33 and p34.
sources.key_finding: Human rights commissions gained public salience after the 2011 constitutional reform, but public perceptions of their effectiveness remain mixed.

sources.citation: Seligson, M. A., & Carrión, J. F. (2002). Political support, political order, and the 'iron fist' in Latin America. In M. A. Seligson (Ed.), The political culture of democracy in Bolivia, 2002. LAPOP, Vanderbilt University.
sources.relevance: Develops the concept of 'mano dura' (iron fist) attitudes in Latin America, measuring citizen willingness to trade civil liberties for security, directly paralleling p7 and p9.
sources.key_finding: Support for authoritarian security measures is higher among citizens who perceive high crime rates and low institutional effectiveness.

sources.citation: Romero, V., Magaloni, B., & Díaz-Cayeros, A. (2016). The Mexican war on drugs: Crime, violence, and institutional weakness. In D. Malone, U. Mehra, & S. Verma (Eds.), The Oxford handbook of Indian foreign policy. Oxford University Press.
sources.relevance: Analyzes how the Mexican drug war context shapes public tolerance for extrajudicial violence and torture by security forces, relevant to p16 and p17.
sources.key_finding: Exposure to drug-related violence increased public acceptance of heavy-handed security tactics, even when these involved human rights violations.

sources.citation: Malone, M. F. T. (2010). The verdict is in: The impact of crime on public trust in Central American justice systems. Journal of Politics in Latin America, 2(3), 99–128.
sources.relevance: Examines the relationship between crime victimization, fear of crime, and support for extralegal justice measures in Latin America.
sources.key_finding: Crime victimization increases support for punitive and extralegal measures, reflecting a security-rights tradeoff in public opinion.

sources.citation: Zizumbo-Colunga, D. (2017). Community, authorities, and support for vigilantism: Experimental evidence. Political Behavior, 39(4), 989–1015.
sources.relevance: Tests experimentally how Mexican citizens justify rights-violating security practices when formal institutions are perceived as ineffective.
sources.key_finding: Weak institutional trust and high crime salience significantly increase support for vigilante justice and tolerance of police violence.

sources.citation: Davis, D. E. (2006). Undermining the rule of law: Democratization and the dark side of police reform in Mexico. Latin American Politics and Society, 48(1), 55–86.
sources.relevance: Discusses how democratic transitions can paradoxically increase tolerance for coercive policing when security deteriorates, contextualizing the rights-security tradeoff.
sources.key_finding: Police reform failures in Mexico led to public frustration that increased tolerance for authoritarian security practices.

sources.citation: Solís, P., Krozer, A., Arroyo Batista, C., & Güémez Graniel, B. (2019). Discriminación étnico-racial en México: Una taxonomía de las prácticas. Colegio de México, Working Paper.
sources.relevance: Directly measures discrimination based on skin color and physical appearance in Mexico, mapping onto p35_1, p39, and p44.
sources.key_finding: Dark-skinned Mexicans report significantly higher rates of discriminatory treatment in public spaces, employment, and housing.

sources.citation: Telles, E. E., & PERLA (Project on Ethnicity and Race in Latin America). (2014). Pigmentocracies: Ethnicity, race, and color in Latin America. University of North Carolina Press.
sources.relevance: Provides comparative evidence on colorism and skin-color-based discrimination in Latin America, including Mexico, relevant to p39.
sources.key_finding: Skin color is a strong predictor of socioeconomic outcomes and perceived discrimination across Latin American countries, including Mexico.

sources.citation: Díez, J. (2015). The politics of gay marriage in Latin America: Argentina, Chile, and Mexico. Cambridge University Press.
sources.relevance: Analyzes attitudes toward homosexuality and LGBTQ+ rights in Mexico, directly relevant to p40_1 and p41.
sources.key_finding: Acceptance of homosexuality in Mexico has increased but remains stratified by religiosity, education, and urban/rural residence.

sources.citation: CONAPRED. (2018). Encuesta Nacional sobre Discriminación 2017 (ENADIS): Principales resultados. Consejo Nacional para Prevenir la Discriminación.
sources.relevance: Mexico's national discrimination survey provides direct measurement parallels for all items in this construct, including discrimination by appearance, religion, and sexual orientation.
sources.key_finding: Over 20% of Mexicans reported experiencing discrimination in the past year, with physical appearance and skin color among the most common bases.

sources.citation: Magaloni, B., Díaz-Cayeros, A., & Estévez, F. (2007). Clientelism and portfolio diversification: A model of electoral investment with applications to Mexico. In H. Kitschelt & S. Wilkinson (Eds.), Patrons, clients, and policies (pp. 182–205). Cambridge University Press.
sources.relevance: Discusses how citizens' lived experience of rights deprivation (including labor rights) shapes political behavior and demands for accountability in Mexico.
sources.key_finding: Personal experience of rights violations increases demand for institutional accountability but also makes citizens vulnerable to clientelistic exchanges.

sources.citation: Hiskey, J. T., & Bowler, S. (2005). Local context and democratization in Mexico. American Journal of Political Science, 49(1), 57–71.
sources.relevance: Examines how personal experiences with authority abuse and institutional failure shape political attitudes at the local level in Mexico, relevant to p5 and p45.
sources.key_finding: Direct negative experiences with authorities reduce political participation and trust, creating a vicious cycle of disengagement.

sources.citation: Bensusán, G. (2006). Diseño legal y desempeño real: Instituciones laborales en América Latina. In G. Bensusán (Ed.), Diseño legal y desempeño real: Instituciones laborales en América Latina. Porrúa/UAM.
sources.relevance: Analyzes the gap between formal labor rights and their enforcement in Mexico, directly relevant to p28_1 on unpaid overtime.
sources.key_finding: Widespread labor rights violations in Mexico reflect weak enforcement mechanisms rather than lack of legal protections.

sources.citation: Vega López, M. G., González Pérez, G. J., & Quintero Vega, P. P. (2013). Ciberacoso: Victimización de alumnos en escuelas secundarias públicas de Tlaquepaque, Jalisco, México. Educación y Desarrollo, 25, 13–20.
sources.relevance: Studies bullying victimization in Mexican schools, relevant to p53 on school bullying experience.
sources.key_finding: School bullying is prevalent in Mexican public schools and is associated with lower academic performance and psychological distress.

sources.citation: Pando Moreno, M., Aranda Beltrán, C., Preciado Serrano, M. L., Franco Chávez, S. A., & Salazar Estrada, J. G. (2006). Validez y confiabilidad del inventario de violencia y acoso psicológico en el trabajo (IVAPT-PANDO). Enseñanza e Investigación en Psicología, 11(2), 319–332.
sources.relevance: Validates a workplace harassment (mobbing) instrument in Mexico, directly relevant to p54.
sources.key_finding: Workplace mobbing in Mexico is measurable and prevalent, with significant effects on worker health and job satisfaction.

research_reviews.gap_justification: While individual components (bullying, mobbing, labor violations, authority abuse) have been studied separately, the construct of bundling diverse personal experiences of rights violations into a single latent dimension is relatively novel. This composite approach is important because it captures the cumulative burden of rights deprivation that shapes citizens' overall sense of vulnerability and protection. In the Mexican context, where multiple forms of rights violations co-occur and compound each other—from workplace exploitation to school violence to police abuse—measuring this lived dimension fills a gap between abstract rights attitudes and concrete institutional evaluations. This construct is essential for understanding how personal biography shapes political attitudes in a context of widespread institutional weakness.

sources.citation: Flamand, L., & Moreno-Jaimes, C. (2015). Do local governments matter? How democratic competition and institutional capacity shape social spending and social outcomes in Mexico's municipalities. In G. Zaremberg (Ed.), Rethinking Latin American social movements, radical action, and institutional politics. FLACSO.
sources.relevance: Examines variation in public service quality (health, education, housing) across Mexican municipalities, directly relevant to p30, p31, and p32.
sources.key_finding: Democratic competition at the local level improves social service delivery, but institutional capacity remains a binding constraint.

sources.citation: Levy, S. (2008). Good intentions, bad outcomes: Social policy, informality, and economic growth in Mexico. Brookings Institution Press.
sources.relevance: Analyzes the structural failures of Mexico's social rights provision system, including health services and housing, relevant to all three items.
sources.key_finding: Mexico's fragmented social protection system creates a two-tier structure where quality of public health and education varies dramatically by employment status.

sources.citation: Scott, J. (2014). Redistributive constraints under high inequality: The case of Mexico. In G. A. Cornia (Ed.), Falling inequality in Latin America: Policy changes and lessons (pp. 150–182). Oxford University Press.
sources.relevance: Discusses how inequality shapes access to and quality of social services in Mexico, providing context for citizens' evaluations of social rights fulfillment.
sources.key_finding: Public spending on health and education in Mexico is regressive, with higher-income groups receiving disproportionate benefits from public services.

sources.citation: Díaz Langou, G., Bezem, P., Aulicino, C., Cano, E., & Sánchez, B. (2014). Los modelos de política social en América Latina y el papel de las organizaciones de la sociedad civil. CIPPEC Working Paper.
sources.relevance: Provides a comparative framework for evaluating social rights provision in Latin America, including health, education, and housing as core social rights.
sources.key_finding: Citizens' evaluations of social service quality are strongly shaped by expectations set by constitutional social rights guarantees.

variable_strategies.justification: Institutional trust in justice is inherently multi-dimensional, spanning prosecutorial performance, victim treatment, judicial confidence, and human rights commission effectiveness; no single item captures the full construct. Questions p18, p19, p20, and p34 all use 6-7 point evaluative/confidence scales that are sufficiently compatible for PCA, which will extract the dominant shared variance reflecting generalized trust in justice institutions. Questions p21 and p33 are excluded because p21 is a binary perception item and p33 is a multiple-choice opinion item about functions rather than trust, making them dimensionally incompatible with the evaluative scale items.

The authoritarian security tradeoff construct captures a latent disposition toward accepting rights violations in the name of security, which is best measured by combining items on police violence, torture acceptance, and the rights-as-obstacle perception. p16 and p17 are both agreement scales (7-8 points) tapping willingness to endorse state coercion, while p9 (5-point yes/no-style scale) directly measures the core trade-off framing; PCA will identify the shared authoritarian-security dimension across these items. p7 is excluded because its two-choice forced comparison format is dimensionally distinct from the agreement scales and would distort the composite.

Perceived discrimination and social exclusion is a multi-dimensional construct spanning racial, sexual orientation, and appearance-based exclusion, requiring aggregation to capture its breadth. p39 (6-point skin-color treatment scale), p40_1 (5-point tolerance scale), and p41 (7-point penalization agreement scale) all use ordinal evaluative or agreement scales of comparable range and together cover the core attitudinal dimensions of discrimination tolerance; PCA will extract the dominant prejudice-exclusion factor. p35_1, p36_1, and p44 are excluded because they are binary or near-binary items with very low entropy (H ≤ 1.23) that would contribute little discriminating information and are better characterized as behavioral rather than attitudinal measures.

p45 — whether the respondent believes their own human rights have ever been violated — is the gold-standard face-valid measure of personal rights experience, directly operationalizing the construct name as a first-person experiential judgment. The remaining items tap narrow, domain-specific victimization experiences (labor overtime, bullying, mobbing) or a general protection perception (p5), which are heterogeneous in content and scale and would not aggregate coherently into a single rights-awareness dimension. A single best-match item avoids conflating distinct victimization domains and provides a clean, interpretable bridge variable.

Social rights service quality is best captured by combining evaluations of public health and public education services, as both are core social rights domains measured on identical 7-point efficiency/quality scales, making them directly combinable via a mean score. p31 (dignified housing perception) is excluded because it uses a 5-point yes/no-style format that is scale-incompatible with the 7-point evaluative items and taps housing conditions rather than service quality per se. The mean of p30 and p32 yields a parsimonious, internally consistent composite of public service quality that is well-suited for the bridge regression.

construct_clusters.name: corruption_perception
construct_clusters.description: Measures the respondent's subjective perception of the prevalence, trajectory, and location of corruption in Mexico, capturing both temporal assessments (past and future) and spatial attributions (where corruption is most concentrated).

construct_clusters.name: tolerance_of_corrupt_behavior
construct_clusters.description: Measures the degree to which respondents normalize or accept corrupt and rule-breaking behaviors, including agreement with pro-corruption rationalizations, willingness to pay bribes, and comfort with minor civic violations.

construct_clusters.name: civic_enforcement_norms
construct_clusters.description: Measures respondents' beliefs about how rule violations should be sanctioned and their personal behavioral responses when witnessing misconduct, reflecting internalized norms around accountability and social enforcement.

construct_clusters.name: institutional_trust_and_efficacy
construct_clusters.description: Measures trust in and perceived effectiveness of institutions in combating corruption, including honesty ratings of public figures, ratings of institutional anti-corruption effort, and awareness of where corruption can be reported.

construct_clusters.name: reporting_behavior_and_barriers
construct_clusters.description: Measures actual behavior and motivations related to reporting corruption and authority abuse, including whether incidents were reported, reasons for non-reporting, and outcomes when reports were made.

sources.citation: Morris, S. D. (2009). Political corruption in Mexico: The impact of democratization. Lynne Rienner Publishers.
sources.relevance: Provides a comprehensive framework for understanding how Mexican citizens perceive corruption across institutions and over time, directly relevant to temporal and spatial attributions of corruption.
sources.key_finding: Democratization in Mexico did not reduce perceived corruption; instead, greater media freedom and political competition heightened public awareness and perception of corruption's prevalence.

sources.citation: Seligson, M. A. (2006). The measurement and impact of corruption victimization: Survey evidence from Latin America. World Development, 34(2), 381–404.
sources.relevance: Discusses methodological approaches to measuring corruption perception versus corruption victimization in Latin American surveys, directly informing how subjective perception items should be interpreted.
sources.key_finding: Corruption perception and corruption victimization are empirically distinct constructs; perception is shaped by media exposure and political attitudes beyond personal experience.

sources.citation: Bailey, J., & Paras, P. (2006). Perceptions and attitudes about corruption and democracy in Mexico. Mexican Studies/Estudios Mexicanos, 22(1), 57–82.
sources.relevance: Directly examines Mexican public opinion data on corruption perceptions, including temporal assessments and institutional attributions, closely mirroring the survey items in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Mexican citizens consistently perceive corruption as worsening over time and concentrate their attributions on government institutions, particularly police and political parties.

sources.citation: Canache, D., & Allison, M. E. (2005). Perceptions of political corruption in Latin American democracies. Latin American Politics and Society, 47(3), 91–111.
sources.relevance: Examines cross-national variation in corruption perceptions in Latin America, providing comparative context for Mexican-specific findings.
sources.key_finding: Corruption perceptions are strongly associated with evaluations of democratic performance and economic conditions, suggesting perceptions capture broader dissatisfaction beyond actual corruption levels.

sources.citation: Transparency International. (2023). Corruption Perceptions Index 2023. Transparency International.
sources.relevance: Provides the most widely used global benchmark for corruption perception measurement, offering methodological parallels to the survey items measuring perceived prevalence and trajectory.
sources.key_finding: Mexico consistently scores in the lower third of the CPI, reflecting persistent high levels of perceived corruption among both citizens and expert assessors.

sources.citation: Manzetti, L., & Wilson, C. J. (2007). Why do corrupt governments maintain public support? Comparative Political Studies, 40(8), 949–970.
sources.relevance: Examines why citizens tolerate corruption, including rationalization mechanisms and clientelistic exchanges, directly relevant to items measuring agreement with pro-corruption statements.
sources.key_finding: Citizens tolerate corruption when they perceive direct material benefits from clientelistic networks, suggesting tolerance is instrumentally rational rather than purely cultural.

sources.citation: Guerrero Gutiérrez, E. (2004). Cultura de la legalidad en México. In F. Carbonell (Ed.), Hacia una cultura de la legalidad. UNAM.
sources.relevance: Directly addresses the Mexican context of 'cultura de la legalidad' and how normalization of minor rule-breaking (like traffic violations) connects to broader tolerance of corruption.
sources.key_finding: Tolerance of minor civic violations in Mexico forms a continuum with tolerance of larger corrupt acts, suggesting a unified cultural dimension of legality.

sources.citation: Bohara, A. K., Mitchell, N. J., & Mittendorff, C. F. (2004). Compound democracy and the control of corruption: A cross-country investigation. Policy Studies Journal, 32(4), 481–499.
sources.relevance: Provides theoretical grounding for how individual-level tolerance of corruption aggregates into societal norms that sustain corrupt systems.
sources.key_finding: Countries with higher individual-level tolerance of rule-breaking exhibit weaker institutional controls on corruption, creating a feedback loop between cultural norms and institutional performance.

sources.citation: Zalpa, G., Tapia, E., & Reyes, J. (2014). 'El que no transa no avanza': La cultura de la corrupción en México. Culturales, 2(1), 145–172.
sources.relevance: Directly examines the Mexican saying 'el que no transa no avanza' (those who don't cheat don't get ahead), which corresponds to survey item p13_1, analyzing its cultural embeddedness.
sources.key_finding: The proverb reflects a deeply internalized cultural schema in which corruption is perceived as a survival strategy rather than a moral failing, particularly among lower socioeconomic groups.

sources.citation: Bicchieri, C., & Xiao, E. (2009). Do the right thing: But only if others do so. Journal of Behavioral Decision Making, 22(2), 191–208.
sources.relevance: Provides theoretical grounding for how social norms around sanctioning deviant behavior operate, relevant to items measuring preferred punishments and bystander responses.
sources.key_finding: People's willingness to enforce norms is conditional on their belief that others also enforce them, creating threshold effects in social norm compliance.

sources.citation: Fehr, E., & Fischbacher, U. (2004). Third-party punishment and social norms. Evolution and Human Behavior, 25(2), 63–87.
sources.relevance: Examines third-party punishment behavior, directly relevant to survey items asking what respondents would do when witnessing theft or cheating by others.
sources.key_finding: Third-party punishment is a robust behavioral phenomenon that sustains cooperation; its strength varies with perceived norm violation severity and institutional context.

sources.citation: Almond, G. A., & Verba, S. (1963). The civic culture: Political attitudes and democracy in five nations. Princeton University Press.
sources.relevance: Classic work on civic culture that frames how internalized norms of accountability and social enforcement relate to democratic functioning, including Mexico as a case study.
sources.key_finding: Mexico exhibited a 'subject' political culture with low levels of civic engagement and norm enforcement, contrasting with the 'participant' cultures of established democracies.

sources.citation: Méndez, I., & Loewe, D. (2016). Cultura de la legalidad y derechos humanos en México. Perfiles Latinoamericanos, 24(48), 13–40.
sources.relevance: Examines the relationship between legal culture, norm enforcement, and human rights in Mexico, providing direct contextual relevance for how Mexicans conceptualize sanctioning behavior.
sources.key_finding: Weak civic enforcement norms in Mexico are associated with distrust in formal institutions, leading citizens to prefer informal or lenient sanctions over formal legal processes.

sources.citation: Mishler, W., & Rose, R. (2001). What are the origins of political trust? Testing institutional and cultural theories in post-communist societies. Comparative Political Studies, 34(1), 30–62.
sources.relevance: Provides the theoretical framework for distinguishing institutional versus cultural origins of political trust, directly relevant to measuring trust in anti-corruption institutions.
sources.key_finding: Institutional trust is primarily driven by institutional performance evaluations rather than cultural predispositions, suggesting that perceived anti-corruption efficacy shapes trust.

sources.citation: Morris, S. D., & Klesner, J. L. (2010). Corruption and trust: Theoretical considerations and evidence from Mexico. Comparative Political Studies, 43(10), 1258–1285.
sources.relevance: Directly examines the relationship between corruption and institutional trust in Mexico using survey data, closely paralleling the construct's measurement approach.
sources.key_finding: Corruption experience and perception both erode institutional trust in Mexico, but the effect of perception is stronger, suggesting that subjective assessments of institutional honesty matter more than direct victimization.

sources.citation: Paras, P. (2007). Cultura política de la democracia en México. LAPOP/Vanderbilt University.
sources.relevance: Uses Mexican survey data to measure trust in institutions including religious figures, government, and civil society, directly paralleling items like honesty ratings of priests and government anti-corruption effort.
sources.key_finding: Mexicans exhibit differentiated trust across institutions, with higher trust in religious and military institutions and lower trust in political parties and police, reflecting selective institutional credibility.

sources.citation: Rothstein, B. (2011). The quality of government: Corruption, social trust, and inequality in international perspective. University of Chicago Press.
sources.relevance: Theorizes the relationship between government quality, corruption control, and social trust, providing the causal logic linking institutional anti-corruption efficacy to broader trust.
sources.key_finding: Impartial and effective government institutions generate social trust, while perceived corruption in public institutions destroys both institutional and generalized social trust.

sources.citation: Olken, B. A. (2007). Monitoring corruption: Evidence from a field experiment in Indonesia. Journal of Political Economy, 115(2), 200–249.
sources.relevance: Examines the behavioral dynamics of corruption reporting and monitoring, providing theoretical grounding for understanding why citizens do or do not report corruption.
sources.key_finding: Community-based monitoring increases reporting when institutional channels are perceived as responsive, but fear of retaliation and perceived futility remain major barriers.

sources.citation: Arias, E. D. (2009). Drugs and democracy in Rio de Janeiro: Trafficking, social networks, and public security. University of North Carolina Press.
sources.relevance: While focused on Brazil, provides transferable insights about why citizens in Latin American contexts remain silent about institutional abuse, relevant to items on authority abuse reactions.
sources.key_finding: Citizens' silence about institutional abuse is driven by rational calculations about personal safety and the perceived complicity of authorities, not merely apathy.

sources.citation: INEGI. (2021). Encuesta Nacional de Victimización y Percepción sobre Seguridad Pública (ENVIPE). Instituto Nacional de Estadística y Geografía.
sources.relevance: Mexico's national victimization survey measures the 'cifra negra' (dark figure) of unreported crime and corruption, providing direct empirical context for non-reporting behavior.
sources.key_finding: Over 90% of crimes and corruption acts in Mexico go unreported, with the primary reasons being perceived futility of reporting, distrust in authorities, and fear of retaliation.

sources.citation: Tavits, M. (2010). Why do people engage in corruption? The case of Estonia. Social Forces, 88(3), 1257–1279.
sources.relevance: Examines individual-level determinants of engagement with and reporting of corruption, providing a micro-level behavioral framework applicable to the Mexican context.
sources.key_finding: Social norms and perceived prevalence of corruption significantly predict both engagement in and reporting of corrupt acts; when corruption is perceived as widespread, reporting decreases.

causal_relationships.related_construct: fear of retaliation / public security perceptions

variable_strategies.justification: Corruption perception is inherently multi-dimensional, spanning retrospective assessment, future expectations, and perceived intractability, so no single item captures it fully. p2, p3, and p34 share a conceptually coherent attitudinal core—all tap the respondent's overall pessimism/optimism about corruption as a societal phenomenon—and their ordinal scales (worse/same/better type) are sufficiently compatible for PCA extraction of a latent severity dimension. p15 is excluded due to near-zero entropy (H=0.46, essentially a constant), and p16/p17 measure institutional location rather than perceived severity, making them construct-misaligned for a perception intensity variable.

Tolerance of corrupt behavior is best captured by attitudinal items that directly ask whether rule-bending is acceptable or constitutes corruption, rather than behavioral or motivational items. p10_1 (tolerating traffic violations), p13_1 (endorsing the 'cheaters prosper' norm), and p9_1 (classifying personal phone use as corruption) all use agree/disagree or yes/no scales measuring normative acceptance of minor infractions, making them scale-compatible and conceptually coherent for a latent tolerance factor. p14, p12, and p19 are excluded because they measure reported behavior or attributed motivations rather than personal normative tolerance, introducing construct contamination.

p23 has the highest Shannon entropy (H=2.57) among all civic enforcement items and is the gold-standard face-valid measure: it asks what the respondent actually did when peers cheated on an exam, directly operationalizing the willingness to enforce norms in a personally relevant social context. The remaining items span highly heterogeneous response formats—hypothetical punishment recommendations for others (p20–p22) versus self-reported behavioral reactions (p24, p11)—mixing normative prescription with behavioral description in ways that make aggregation conceptually muddled. A single high-entropy behavioral item avoids scale incompatibility and provides a clean, actionable bridge variable.

Institutional trust and efficacy is best operationalized by combining the two 0–10 numeric rating scales (p6_1: honesty of priests as a civil-society anchor; p36_1: government effectiveness against corruption), which are directly scale-compatible and together span both societal and governmental trust dimensions. Averaging these two items yields a continuous latent trust-efficacy score that can be binned into ordinal categories for the bridge model. p25_1 and p18 measure knowledge of reporting channels and perceived enforcement location rather than trust intensity, and p35_1 has very low entropy (H=0.97) and taps responsibility attribution rather than trust, so all three are excluded to preserve construct validity.

p32—reasons for staying silent about authority abuse—is the gold-standard measure of reporting barriers because it directly captures the motivational and structural obstacles that suppress reporting, which is the theoretically central mechanism linking SES to under-reporting of corruption. While p33 has higher entropy (H=2.52), it conditions on having reported, making it applicable only to a small sub-sample and introducing severe selection bias in a bridge regression. Aggregating across p27–p33 is inappropriate because the items form a conditional funnel (victimization → reporting → barriers → outcomes) with incompatible scales and drastically different eligible sub-populations at each step.

construct_clusters.name: structural_housing_quality
construct_clusters.description: Measures the physical quality of the dwelling's basic construction materials — walls, roof, and floor — as indicators of structural adequacy and habitability. Together these items form a composite index of material precariousness.

construct_clusters.name: basic_services_access
construct_clusters.description: Captures the availability and adequacy of essential infrastructure services within the dwelling, including water supply frequency and delivery method, drainage, and electricity, reflecting the degree to which households are connected to public utility networks.

construct_clusters.name: household_asset_endowment
construct_clusters.description: Measures the stock of durable goods and domestic appliances present in the dwelling — from basic items like refrigerators and washing machines to modern assets like computers and solar panels — as a proxy for household socioeconomic status and living standards.

construct_clusters.name: housing_tenure_security
construct_clusters.description: Measures the legal and practical security of the household's occupancy arrangement, including ownership status, who holds title, how the dwelling was acquired, and whether it was obtained through a formal financing institution.

construct_clusters.name: housing_problem_perception
construct_clusters.description: Captures respondents' subjective evaluation of housing conditions in Mexico at a societal level, including their overall quality rating, perceived primary solutions, and attribution of institutional responsibility for resolving housing problems.

sources.citation: CONEVAL. (2018). Metodología para la medición multidimensional de la pobreza en México (3ra ed.). Consejo Nacional de Evaluación de la Política de Desarrollo Social.
sources.relevance: CONEVAL's multidimensional poverty methodology explicitly uses wall, roof, and floor materials as indicators of the 'quality and spaces of the dwelling' dimension of social deprivation, directly paralleling this construct.
sources.key_finding: Dwellings with precarious materials (dirt floors, cardboard/waste roofing, makeshift walls) are classified as deprived in the housing quality indicator, affecting roughly 12% of the Mexican population.

sources.citation: UN-Habitat. (2003). The challenge of slums: Global report on human settlements 2003. United Nations Human Settlements Programme, Earthscan Publications.
sources.relevance: Establishes the international framework for measuring housing adequacy, including structural durability of construction materials as a core dimension of slum/non-slum classification.
sources.key_finding: Structural durability of housing materials is one of five deprivation indicators used to define slum households globally, and composite material indices predict health and well-being outcomes.

sources.citation: Mier y Terán, A., Vázquez, I., & Ziccardi, A. (2012). Pobreza urbana, segregación residencial y mejoramiento del espacio público en la Ciudad de México. Sociologias, 14(30), 118–155.
sources.relevance: Examines how structural housing quality in Mexican urban areas relates to spatial segregation and poverty, using material composition as a key indicator of habitability.
sources.key_finding: Precarious construction materials cluster spatially in peripheral urban zones, reinforcing socio-spatial segregation and limiting access to public services.

sources.citation: Garza, G., & Schteingart, M. (2010). Los grandes problemas de México, Vol. II: Desarrollo urbano y regional. El Colegio de México.
sources.relevance: Provides a comprehensive analysis of housing conditions in Mexico, including material quality indices as central measures of the housing deficit.
sources.key_finding: Mexico's qualitative housing deficit—dwellings with inadequate materials but existing structures—far exceeds the quantitative deficit, highlighting the importance of material-based quality measurement.

sources.citation: Cattaneo, M. D., Galiani, S., Gertler, P. J., Martinez, S., & Titiunik, R. (2009). Housing, health, and happiness. American Economic Journal: Economic Policy, 1(1), 75–105.
sources.relevance: Evaluates Mexico's Piso Firme program replacing dirt floors with cement, directly measuring the health and well-being impacts of one structural housing material indicator.
sources.key_finding: Replacing dirt floors with cement floors significantly reduced parasitic infestations, diarrhea, and anemia in children, and improved adult mental health and life satisfaction.

sources.citation: CONEVAL. (2018). Metodología para la medición multidimensional de la pobreza en México (3ra ed.). Consejo Nacional de Evaluación de la Política de Desarrollo Social.
sources.relevance: Access to basic services in the dwelling (water, drainage, electricity) constitutes one of the six social deprivation indicators in Mexico's official multidimensional poverty measurement.
sources.key_finding: Approximately 19.8% of the Mexican population was deprived in access to basic housing services as of 2018, with drainage and piped water being the most common deficiencies.

sources.citation: Jiménez, A., Cortobius, M., & Kjellén, M. (2014). Water, sanitation and hygiene and indigenous peoples: A review of the literature. Water International, 39(3), 277–293.
sources.relevance: Reviews the relationship between basic water and sanitation service access and indigenous populations, relevant to Mexico's significant indigenous housing service gaps.
sources.key_finding: Indigenous communities consistently face disproportionate lack of access to water and sanitation services, driven by geographic isolation, discrimination, and institutional neglect.

sources.citation: Soares, D. (2007). Acceso, abasto y control del agua en una comunidad indígena chamula en Chiapas. Un análisis a través de la perspectiva de género, ambiente y desarrollo. Región y Sociedad, 19(38), 25–50.
sources.relevance: Examines water access patterns in marginalized Mexican communities, showing how service delivery frequency and method shape daily life and gender dynamics.
sources.key_finding: Intermittent water supply forces women to dedicate significant time to water collection, reinforcing gender inequalities and limiting economic participation.

sources.citation: Boltvinik, J. (2003). Tipología de los métodos de medición de la pobreza. Los métodos combinados. Comercio Exterior, 53(5), 453–463.
sources.relevance: Discusses combined poverty measurement methods in Latin America that integrate basic service access indicators (water, drainage, electricity) as core components of unsatisfied basic needs (NBI).
sources.key_finding: The NBI approach using service access indicators captures structural poverty dimensions that income-based measures miss, making it essential for comprehensive poverty assessment in Mexico.

causal_relationships.related_construct: waterborne disease and child health outcomes
municipal governance and federalism (public investment allocation)

sources.citation: Filmer, D., & Pritchett, L. H. (2001). Estimating wealth effects without expenditure data—or tears: An application to educational enrollments in states of India. Demography, 38(1), 115–132.
sources.relevance: Foundational paper establishing the asset index methodology using durable goods ownership as a proxy for household wealth, widely adopted in developing country surveys including Mexico.
sources.key_finding: A principal-components-based asset index using household durable goods produces wealth rankings highly correlated with consumption expenditure and predicts educational enrollment as well as direct expenditure measures.

sources.citation: McKenzie, D. J. (2005). Measuring inequality with asset indicators. Journal of Population Economics, 18(2), 229–260.
sources.relevance: Critically evaluates asset-based wealth indices in developing countries, including Mexico, discussing which durable goods best discriminate across the wealth distribution.
sources.key_finding: Asset indices perform well at distinguishing the poorest households but compress differences at the top of the distribution; combining diverse asset types (appliances, vehicles, electronics) improves discriminatory power.

sources.citation: AMAI (Asociación Mexicana de Agencias de Inteligencia de Mercado y Opinión). (2018). Nivel Socio Económico AMAI 2018: Nota metodológica. AMAI.
sources.relevance: The AMAI socioeconomic classification system used extensively in Mexican market research and public opinion surveys relies heavily on household asset ownership to classify households into socioeconomic strata.
sources.key_finding: Ownership of specific assets (automobile, computer, internet access, number of bathrooms) are among the strongest predictors of socioeconomic level in Mexico's AMAI classification.

sources.citation: Vyas, S., & Kumaranayake, L. (2006). Constructing socio-economic status indices: How to use principal components analysis. Health Policy and Planning, 21(6), 459–468.
sources.relevance: Provides methodological guidance on constructing asset-based socioeconomic indices from binary durable goods indicators, directly applicable to the items in this construct.
sources.key_finding: PCA-based asset indices are robust proxies for socioeconomic status when consumption data are unavailable, though item selection and urban-rural context significantly affect index validity.

causal_relationships.related_construct: access to information and digital inclusion

sources.citation: Durand-Lasserve, A., & Selod, H. (2009). The formalization of urban land tenure in developing countries. In S. V. Lall, M. Freire, B. Yuen, R. Rajack, & J.-J. Helluin (Eds.), Urban land markets: Improving land management for successful urbanization (pp. 101–132). Springer.
sources.relevance: Analyzes the relationship between formal tenure security and housing investment in developing countries, directly relevant to Mexico's mix of formal and informal tenure arrangements.
sources.key_finding: Formal tenure security significantly increases household investment in housing improvements, but the magnitude depends on the credibility of enforcement and local institutional context.

sources.citation: Monkkonen, P. (2011). The housing transition in Mexico: Expanding access to housing finance. Urban Affairs Review, 47(5), 672–695.
sources.relevance: Examines Mexico's housing finance system (INFONAVIT, FOVISSSTE) and how institutional acquisition channels shape tenure patterns, directly relevant to the financing institution question (p18).
sources.key_finding: Mexico's expansion of formal housing finance through INFONAVIT dramatically increased homeownership rates but produced peripheral, low-quality developments disconnected from employment centers.

sources.citation: Salazar Cruz, C. E. (2012). Los ejidatarios en el control de la regularización. In A. Azuela (Ed.), La ciudad y sus reglas: Sobre la huella del derecho en el orden urbano. UNAM/PAOT.
sources.relevance: Examines informal land tenure and regularization processes in Mexican ejidal (communal) lands, relevant to understanding the spectrum of tenure security captured by this construct.
sources.key_finding: Informal tenure on ejidal land creates a continuum of security rather than a binary formal/informal distinction, with perceived security often mattering more than legal title for housing investment decisions.

sources.citation: De Soto, H. (2000). The mystery of capital: Why capitalism triumphs in the West and fails everywhere else. Basic Books.
sources.relevance: Influential theoretical framework arguing that formal property rights (tenure security) unlock capital for the poor, widely debated in the Mexican housing policy context.
sources.key_finding: Formalization of property rights transforms 'dead capital' in informal housing into assets that can be leveraged for credit, though empirical evidence in Mexico has been mixed.

causal_relationships.related_construct: access to formal credit and financial inclusion

sources.citation: Ziccardi, A. (2015). Cómo viven los mexicanos: Análisis regional de las condiciones de habitabilidad de la vivienda. UNAM, Instituto de Investigaciones Jurídicas.
sources.relevance: Directly analyzes Mexican perceptions of housing conditions and habitability, including subjective evaluations of dwelling quality and satisfaction, closely matching this construct's items.
sources.key_finding: Subjective housing satisfaction in Mexico diverges significantly from objective indicators, with many residents of objectively precarious dwellings reporting acceptable satisfaction levels, suggesting adaptation effects.

sources.citation: Adriaanse, C. C. M. (2007). Measuring residential satisfaction: A residential environmental satisfaction scale (RESS). Journal of Housing and the Built Environment, 22(3), 287–304.
sources.relevance: Develops a validated scale for measuring residential satisfaction that includes both dwelling-specific and neighborhood-level items, providing theoretical grounding for combining satisfaction and societal-level perception items.
sources.key_finding: Residential satisfaction is a multidimensional construct where dwelling characteristics, neighborhood social environment, and perceived institutional responsiveness form distinct but correlated factors.

sources.citation: Ibarra Salazar, J., & Sotres Cervantes, L. (2009). Determinantes de la satisfacción con la vivienda en México. Estudios Económicos, 24(1), 51–78.
sources.relevance: Empirically models determinants of housing satisfaction in Mexico using survey data, examining how objective conditions translate into subjective evaluations.
sources.key_finding: Housing satisfaction in Mexico is significantly predicted by dwelling size, construction materials, and service access, but sociodemographic characteristics (age, education) moderate these relationships.

causal_relationships.related_construct: trust in government and institutional performance evaluation
demand for government housing policy intervention
perception of public security in neighborhood
human rights perceptions (right to adequate housing)

research_reviews.gap_justification: While objective housing measurement is well-established in Mexico, the subjective perception of housing problems at a societal level—including attribution of responsibility and preferred solutions—is understudied in the academic literature. Most studies focus on individual residential satisfaction rather than societal-level problem framing. This construct fills an important gap by capturing how Mexican citizens conceptualize housing as a public policy issue, who they hold accountable, and what solutions they prioritize. Understanding these perceptions is critical for analyzing the demand side of housing policy and how housing concerns interact with broader political attitudes, institutional trust, and social policy preferences. The inclusion of both personal satisfaction (p46_1) and societal evaluation (p47) items allows researchers to examine the gap between private experience and public opinion, a distinction central to understanding Mexican political behavior around housing.

variable_strategies.justification: Structural housing quality is inherently multi-dimensional, requiring information on walls, roof, and floor materials to capture the full spectrum of physical dwelling quality—no single item covers it adequately. These three items (p1, p2, p3) share conceptual equivalence as ordinal material-quality scales ranging from precarious to durable materials, making them combinable via PCA to extract the dominant latent dimension of structural adequacy. p28 (dwelling type) and p42_1 (natural/traditional materials) are excluded because they capture dwelling classification and a binary traditional-material flag respectively, which conflate structural quality with dwelling typology and would distort the composite.

Basic services access is a multi-dimensional construct spanning water availability, sewage/drainage, and electricity, and no single question captures all three infrastructure dimensions simultaneously. The selected items—water frequency (p7), drainage type (p10), electric lighting (p11), drainage presence (p36_30), and septic/latrine presence (p36_31)—collectively span the core service domains and can be treated as ordinal indicators of service provision quality suitable for PCA. Items p7_1, p8, and p9 are excluded because they capture delivery modality, unspecified utilities, and exclusivity of use respectively, which are secondary access characteristics that would introduce conceptual noise into the latent services-access dimension.

Household asset endowment is a classic latent wealth construct best captured by summing binary ownership indicators across a diverse asset basket, following the standard asset-index methodology used in DHS and MICS surveys. All selected items share an identical binary (yes/no) scale, making simple summation directly appropriate and interpretable as a count of assets owned, which is then binned into ≤5 ordinal wealth categories. Items with very low entropy (p36_2 television H=0.20, p36_4 blender H=0.50, p36_21 heating H=0.38, p36_22 solar panels H=0.23) are excluded because near-universal or near-zero ownership provides minimal discriminatory power across households and would compress variance in the composite score.

Tenure security is best captured by p15 (tenure status/ownership classification), which directly encodes the legally and socially meaningful tenure categories—owned outright, rented, borrowed, irregular, etc.—that determine a household's security of occupation and is the gold-standard measure used in housing surveys worldwide. Although p14 has higher entropy (H=2.04) and p18 has high entropy (H=1.90), they capture the identity of the owner/lender and the financing institution respectively, which are downstream details conditional on tenure type rather than independent measures of security. p15's eight categories (H=1.71) provide sufficient ordinal variation for the bridge model while maintaining direct face validity as the primary tenure-security indicator.

The five items in this construct span conceptually distinct perceptual dimensions—national housing quality rating, perceived solutions, institutional responsibility, dwelling satisfaction, and neighbor behavior—that are not sufficiently homogeneous to justify aggregation into a single composite score. p50_1 (primary solution to housing problems, H=2.59) is selected as the highest-entropy item and also carries strong face validity as a direct measure of problem salience and perceived housing deficits, reflecting the respondent's diagnosis of the housing situation. Its nine response categories provide the richest distributional spread for the bridge regression, maximizing the information available to link this perceptual construct across surveys.

construct_clusters.name: cosmopolitan_identity
construct_clusters.description: Measures the degree to which respondents identify as global citizens rather than exclusively as nationals, capturing openness to a supranational self-concept versus a Mexico-first orientation.

construct_clusters.name: transnational_exposure
construct_clusters.description: Measures the extent of respondents' direct personal experience with the world beyond Mexico, including international travel, residence abroad, language skills, and close ties to people living in other countries.

construct_clusters.name: cultural_openness_to_foreign_influence
construct_clusters.description: Measures receptivity to foreign cultural content and practices in daily life, including consumption of foreign media, music, food, and attitudes toward the diffusion of foreign ideas and customs in Mexico.

construct_clusters.name: economic_globalization_attitudes
construct_clusters.description: Measures respondents' evaluations of economic integration with the world, including opinions on free trade agreements, foreign investment, and the US-Mexico trade relationship.

construct_clusters.name: international_institutional_engagement
construct_clusters.description: Measures the degree to which respondents support Mexico's participation in and deference to international institutions and multilateral governance, including the UN, NGOs, and international human rights bodies.

sources.citation: Roudometof, V. (2005). Transnationalism, cosmopolitanism and glocalization. Current Sociology, 53(1), 113–135.
sources.relevance: Provides a theoretical framework distinguishing cosmopolitan identity from transnationalism and localism, directly relevant to operationalizing a 'citizen of the world' self-concept.
sources.key_finding: Cosmopolitanism is an analytically distinct orientation from transnationalism, characterized by openness to otherness and a willingness to engage with the 'Other'.

sources.citation: Norris, P., & Inglehart, R. (2009). Cosmopolitan communications: Cultural diversity in a globalized world. Cambridge University Press.
sources.relevance: Examines how cosmopolitan identities form through exposure to global media and cultural flows, linking identity measures to cultural consumption patterns.
sources.key_finding: Cosmopolitan orientations are shaped by both structural exposure to global communications and individual-level value priorities, with education as a key moderator.

sources.citation: Pichler, F. (2009). 'Down-to-earth' cosmopolitanism: Subjective and objective measurements of cosmopolitanism in survey research. Current Sociology, 57(5), 704–732.
sources.relevance: Directly addresses the measurement of cosmopolitan identity in survey research, distinguishing subjective identification from behavioral indicators.
sources.key_finding: Subjective cosmopolitan identity (feeling like a world citizen) and objective cosmopolitan practices (travel, language skills) are related but distinct dimensions requiring separate measurement.

sources.citation: Skey, M. (2012). We need to talk about cosmopolitanism: The challenge of studying openness towards other people. Cultural Sociology, 6(4), 471–487.
sources.relevance: Critically examines how national pride and cosmopolitan identity interact, relevant to the inclusion of Mexican pride items alongside global citizen items.
sources.key_finding: National pride and cosmopolitan identity are not necessarily oppositional; many individuals hold both simultaneously, suggesting a multidimensional identity space.

sources.citation: Flores, R., & Schachter, A. (2018). Who are the 'real' Americans? The boundaries of national identity in a diversifying America. Social Forces, 97(3), 1101–1132.
sources.relevance: Though US-focused, provides methodological insights on measuring national vs. supranational identity boundaries applicable to the Mexican context.
sources.key_finding: Ethnocultural and civic conceptions of national identity coexist and differentially predict attitudes toward immigration and globalization.

sources.citation: Mau, S., Mewes, J., & Zimmermann, A. (2008). Cosmopolitan attitudes through transnational social practices? Global Networks, 8(1), 1–24.
sources.relevance: Directly tests whether transnational practices (travel, foreign contacts, language skills) predict cosmopolitan attitudes, mirroring the items in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Transnational social practices significantly predict cosmopolitan attitudes, with international travel and cross-border social ties being the strongest predictors.

sources.citation: Díez Medrano, J. (2003). Framing Europe: Attitudes to European integration in Germany, Spain, and the United Kingdom. Princeton University Press.
sources.relevance: Examines how personal transnational experiences shape attitudes toward supranational integration, providing a theoretical model applicable to Mexico's globalization context.
sources.key_finding: Direct personal experience with other countries—through travel, residence, and personal networks—shapes frames through which individuals interpret economic and political integration.

sources.citation: Levitt, P. (2001). The transnational villagers. University of California Press.
