sources.key_finding: Resistance to women's political participation in Mexico manifests not only in attitudinal opposition but also in political violence targeting female candidates and officeholders.

causal_relationships.related_construct: women's political participation and voter turnout

research_reviews.gap_justification: While there is substantial literature on gender quotas and women's descriptive representation in Mexico, this construct uniquely combines willingness to vote for women, awareness of equality campaigns, perceptions of gendered life difficulty, and attitudes toward homosexuality. The inclusion of p42_1 (homosexuality as normal) alongside political gender equality items is less commonly studied as a unified construct, though it captures a broader progressive-conservative attitudinal dimension. This combination is important because it measures the breadth of egalitarian attitudes in public life beyond narrow political representation, filling a gap in understanding how tolerance for gender and sexual diversity co-varies with support for women's political participation in Mexican public opinion.

variable_strategies.justification: Traditional gender role attitudes are inherently multi-dimensional, spanning economic, domestic, sexual, and ideological domains, so no single item captures the full construct. All five selected items share the same 7-point agree/disagree Likert scale, making them directly combinable into a mean score; p10_1 is excluded because its reverse-coded, low-entropy (H=1.18) item about equal treatment of boys and girls skews heavily toward social desirability and would compress variance. Mean-scoring followed by binning to ≤5 categories preserves ordinal gradation while satisfying the bridge model's category constraint.

Intimate partner power dynamics is a latent construct best captured by items that directly reflect control and autonomy rather than neutral financial knowledge (p21, p22) or household role assignment (p18, p20_1), which conflate descriptive facts with power imbalance. The four selected items—economic withholding, permission-seeking for outings, permission-seeking for family visits, and perceived financial control—all tap the coercive control dimension of the construct, though their scales differ slightly (K=4 to K=6), making PCA preferable to simple summation as it extracts the shared variance dimension while accommodating scale heterogeneity. The first principal component is then binned into 5 quantile categories for the bridge model.

Tolerance of GBV is best operationalized through attitudinal and normative items rather than experiential or factual ones; p32 (opinion on whether violence exists) has near-zero entropy (H=0.58) and is essentially a factual belief item, p33 measures personal experience rather than tolerance, and p34 captures intended behavioral response rather than normative acceptance. The three retained items—emotional reaction to partner yelling (p31), justification of hitting (p35), and awareness of protective law (p65, reverse-coded as a proxy for normative engagement)—span affective, cognitive-normative, and legal-awareness facets of tolerance, and their 4–8 category ordinal scales are sufficiently compatible for mean-scoring after directional alignment, then binned to 5 categories.

Sexual and reproductive attitudes span premarital sex norms, sex work judgments, and abortion/pregnancy decision-making, requiring aggregation to cover the construct's breadth. Items p46_1 (contraceptive knowledge/use) and p52 (abortion legality knowledge) are factual knowledge items rather than attitudinal measures and are excluded to maintain construct homogeneity; p48 and p49 tap problem perception and causal attribution rather than normative attitudes. The four retained items (p45, p47, p50, p51) all elicit normative opinions on sexual and reproductive scenarios using 5–8 category scales, and PCA is preferred over simple summation because the scales differ in range and the items likely load on a common liberal-versus-conservative reproductive attitudes factor, with the first component binned into 5 quantiles.

Gender equality in public life requires capturing both political inclusion attitudes and broader egalitarian orientations; p66 (media exposure) is a behavioral/environmental item rather than an attitudinal measure and is excluded to preserve construct validity. The three retained items—willingness to vote for a woman (p41_1, K=5), agreement that homosexuality is normal (p42_1, K=7, H=2.37 highest), and perception of whose life is harder by gender (p9, K=6)—collectively index egalitarian public-sphere attitudes across political, social, and comparative dimensions. Despite slight scale differences (K=5 to K=7), the items are sufficiently ordinal and directionally alignable for mean-scoring, with the result binned to 5 categories for the bridge model.

construct_clusters.name: cultural_socialization_in_childhood
construct_clusters.description: Measures the degree to which respondents were exposed to cultural practices and resources during childhood through parental behavior, capturing early cultural capital transmission within the family.

construct_clusters.name: reading_engagement_and_literacy
construct_clusters.description: Measures respondents' current relationship with reading, including access to books, perceived reading ability, self-reported reading habits across publication types, and perceived barriers to reading.

construct_clusters.name: cultural_identity_and_heritage
construct_clusters.description: Measures respondents' sense of cultural belonging and self-identification, including ethnic/racial self-categorization, indigenous or distinct heritage recognition, first language, and identification as part of a discriminated group.

construct_clusters.name: attitudes_toward_cultural_openness_and_foreign_influence
construct_clusters.description: Measures respondents' orientations toward cultural diversity and external cultural influence, including perceptions of foreign cultural impact, preferences for cultural homogeneity versus pluralism, and openness to multicultural education.

construct_clusters.name: sports_engagement_and_media_consumption
construct_clusters.description: Measures respondents' involvement with sports both as active participants and as media audiences, as well as their beliefs about the social function of sport and what motivates youth participation.

sources.citation: Bourdieu, P. (1984). Distinction: A social critique of the judgement of taste. Harvard University Press.
sources.relevance: Foundational theory on cultural capital transmission from parents to children, directly underpinning the concept of early cultural socialization through parental practices such as museum visits, book gifting, and cultural discussions.
sources.key_finding: Cultural capital is primarily transmitted through the family during childhood, reproducing social inequalities across generations.

sources.citation: De Graaf, N. D., De Graaf, P. M., & Kraaykamp, G. (2000). Parental cultural capital and educational attainment in the Netherlands: A refinement of the cultural capital perspective. Sociology of Education, 73(2), 92–111.
sources.relevance: Empirically tests how parental reading behavior and cultural participation during childhood predict children's educational outcomes, closely matching the construct's indicators (parental book gifting, reading frequency, cultural discussions).
sources.key_finding: Parental reading climate in the home is a stronger predictor of children's educational attainment than parents' participation in highbrow cultural activities.

sources.citation: Lareau, A. (2003). Unequal childhoods: Class, race, and family life. University of California Press.
sources.relevance: Documents how middle-class parents engage in 'concerted cultivation' including cultural exposure activities, directly relevant to measuring childhood cultural socialization practices.
sources.key_finding: Social class fundamentally shapes parenting strategies regarding cultural exposure, with middle-class families actively fostering children's cultural development through organized activities and enrichment.

sources.citation: Sullivan, A. (2001). Cultural capital and educational attainment. Sociology, 35(4), 893–912.
sources.relevance: Operationalizes and measures cultural capital transmission in childhood, including reading habits and cultural knowledge acquired through family, aligning with the construct's question items.
sources.key_finding: Cultural capital, measured through reading and cultural activities in the home, has a significant independent effect on educational performance even after controlling for social class.

sources.citation: Kraaykamp, G., & van Eijck, K. (2010). The intergenerational reproduction of cultural capital: A threefold perspective. Social Forces, 89(1), 209–231.
sources.relevance: Examines multiple pathways of intergenerational cultural capital transmission including parental example, parental stimulation, and resource provision—all captured by this construct's indicators.
sources.key_finding: Parental cultural socialization operates through distinct mechanisms (modeling, stimulation, and resource provision), each independently contributing to children's cultural participation in adulthood.

causal_relationships.related_construct: socioeconomic status of family of origin

sources.citation: OECD. (2019). PISA 2018 results (Volume I): What students know and can do. OECD Publishing.
sources.relevance: Provides international benchmarks for reading literacy and engagement, including Mexico-specific data on reading habits, self-perceived reading difficulty, and access to reading materials.
sources.key_finding: Mexican students score significantly below the OECD average in reading, with socioeconomic status and access to books at home being strong predictors of reading performance.

sources.citation: Módulo sobre Lectura (MOLEC), INEGI. (2023). Resultados del Módulo sobre Lectura 2023. Instituto Nacional de Estadística y Geografía.
sources.relevance: Directly measures reading habits in Mexico including book access, newspaper/magazine reading, and perceived barriers to reading—closely matching the construct's question items.
sources.key_finding: The proportion of the Mexican literate population that reads has declined over the past decade, with lack of time and lack of interest cited as primary barriers.

sources.citation: Krashen, S. D. (2004). The power of reading: Insights from the research (2nd ed.). Libraries Unlimited.
sources.relevance: Synthesizes research on free voluntary reading and its relationship to literacy development, relevant to understanding self-reported reading habits and perceived reading difficulty.
sources.key_finding: Access to books and free voluntary reading are among the most powerful predictors of literacy development, reading comprehension, vocabulary, and writing ability.

sources.citation: Clark, C., & Rumbold, K. (2006). Reading for pleasure: A research overview. National Literacy Trust.
sources.relevance: Reviews the relationship between reading engagement, access to reading materials, and perceived barriers—all core components of this construct.
sources.key_finding: Reading for pleasure is strongly associated with reading attainment, and barriers such as perceived difficulty and lack of access significantly reduce engagement.

causal_relationships.related_construct: information society participation and digital literacy

sources.citation: Bonfil Batalla, G. (1996). México profundo: Reclaiming a civilization. University of Texas Press.
sources.relevance: Seminal work on the tension between indigenous and mestizo identities in Mexico, directly relevant to the construct's questions on ethnic self-categorization, indigenous language ability, and heritage recognition.
sources.key_finding: Mexico's dominant mestizo national identity has systematically marginalized indigenous cultural identities, creating a 'deep Mexico' of unrecognized cultural heritage.

sources.citation: Villarreal, A. (2010). Stratification by skin color in contemporary Mexico. American Sociological Review, 75(5), 652–678.
sources.relevance: Examines racial/ethnic self-identification in Mexico and its relationship to social stratification, directly relevant to the construct's racial self-categorization item (p54).
sources.key_finding: Skin color is a significant predictor of socioeconomic outcomes in Mexico, independent of education and other factors, revealing persistent racial stratification.

sources.citation: Martínez Casas, R., Saldívar, E., Flores, R. D., & Sue, C. A. (2015). The different faces of mestizaje: Ethnicity and race in Mexico. In E. Telles (Ed.), Pigmentocracies: Ethnicity, race, and color in Latin America (pp. 36–80). University of North Carolina Press.
sources.relevance: Analyzes how Mexicans self-identify racially and ethnically, including the complexity of indigenous identity recognition and mestizo categorization—core elements of this construct.
sources.key_finding: Ethnic and racial self-identification in Mexico is fluid and context-dependent, with many individuals of indigenous heritage not self-identifying as indigenous due to social stigma.

sources.citation: INALI (Instituto Nacional de Lenguas Indígenas). (2012). México: Lenguas indígenas nacionales en riesgo de desaparición. INALI.
sources.relevance: Documents the status of indigenous languages in Mexico, directly relevant to the construct's items on first language learned and ability to converse/read in indigenous languages.
sources.key_finding: Many of Mexico's 68 indigenous languages are at risk of disappearing, with intergenerational transmission declining as speakers shift to Spanish.

sources.citation: Telles, E., & Garcia, D. (2013). 'Mestizaje' and public opinion in Latin America. Latin American Research Review, 48(3), 130–152.
sources.relevance: Examines how racial/ethnic self-identification relates to attitudes and public opinion across Latin America, including Mexico, connecting identity to broader social attitudes.
sources.key_finding: Racial self-identification significantly predicts attitudes toward discrimination and social policy preferences in Latin America, even after controlling for socioeconomic status.

causal_relationships.related_construct: attitudes toward cultural openness and foreign influence

sources.citation: Inglehart, R., & Baker, W. E. (2000). Modernization, cultural change, and the persistence of traditional values. American Sociological Review, 65(1), 19–51.
sources.relevance: Examines how globalization and modernization shape cultural values and openness to foreign influence, providing theoretical grounding for attitudes toward cultural pluralism versus homogeneity.
sources.key_finding: Economic development drives predictable cultural changes toward greater openness and tolerance, but traditional values persist and cultural heritage continues to shape societies' value systems.

sources.citation: Tomlinson, J. (1999). Globalization and culture. University of Chicago Press.
sources.relevance: Theorizes the relationship between globalization and cultural identity, directly relevant to questions about foreign cultural influence and preferences for cultural preservation versus openness.
sources.key_finding: Globalization does not simply homogenize cultures but creates complex interactions between local and global cultural forces, generating both resistance and hybridization.

sources.citation: García Canclini, N. (1995). Hybrid cultures: Strategies for entering and leaving modernity. University of Minnesota Press.
sources.relevance: Analyzes cultural hybridization in Latin America and Mexico specifically, directly relevant to attitudes about whether Mexico should preserve its culture, adopt foreign elements, or embrace pluralism.
sources.key_finding: Latin American cultures are inherently hybrid, and attempts to preserve 'pure' national cultures ignore the productive cultural mixing that characterizes the region's modernity.

sources.citation: Lizama Quijano, J. (2012). La educación intercultural bilingüe en México: Discursos y prácticas. Desacatos, 38, 9–14.
sources.relevance: Discusses intercultural education policy in Mexico, directly relevant to the construct's items on teaching indigenous languages and multicultural education in primary schools.
sources.key_finding: Intercultural bilingual education in Mexico faces significant implementation gaps between policy discourse promoting cultural diversity and actual classroom practices.

sources.citation: Coakley, J. (2015). Sports in society: Issues and controversies (11th ed.). McGraw-Hill.
sources.relevance: Comprehensive sociological analysis of sports participation, media consumption of sports, and beliefs about the social functions of sport—all core components of this construct.
sources.key_finding: Sports participation and media consumption are shaped by social class, gender, and cultural context, and beliefs about sport's social functions (character building, health, national unity) vary systematically across populations.

sources.citation: INEGI. (2022). Módulo de Práctica Deportiva y Ejercicio Físico (MOPRADEF). Instituto Nacional de Estadística y Geografía.
sources.relevance: Directly measures sports participation patterns in Mexico, including types of sports practiced and frequency, closely matching the construct's indicators.
sources.key_finding: Approximately 39% of the Mexican population aged 18 and over reported practicing sport or physical exercise, with significant variation by age, gender, and socioeconomic status.

sources.citation: Wann, D. L., Melnick, M. J., Russell, G. W., & Pease, D. G. (2001). Sport fans: The psychology and social impact of spectators. Routledge.
sources.relevance: Examines the psychology of sports media consumption and spectatorship, relevant to the construct's items on watching sports on television and preferences for national versus international competitions.
sources.key_finding: Sports media consumption serves multiple psychological and social functions including entertainment, group affiliation, and national identity expression.

sources.citation: Ramírez Rodríguez, J. C. (2014). Deporte, cultura y sociedad en México: Una mirada sociológica. Revista Mexicana de Sociología, 76(especial), 115–140.
sources.relevance: Analyzes the social and cultural dimensions of sport in Mexico, including beliefs about sport's role in youth development and national identity—directly relevant to the construct's attitudinal items.
sources.key_finding: Sport in Mexico functions as a site of social reproduction where class, gender, and regional identities are both expressed and contested.

research_reviews.gap_justification: While sports participation and media consumption are well-studied independently, the combination of active sports engagement, sports media consumption, and beliefs about sport's social function as a unified construct is less commonly operationalized in Mexican public opinion research. This construct fills an important gap by linking behavioral engagement (practicing sport) with media behavior (watching sports) and normative beliefs (what sport is for, what motivates youth), enabling analysis of how sports culture intersects with broader social attitudes. In the Mexican context, where fútbol and other sports carry significant national identity implications and where public investment in sports infrastructure is a policy issue, understanding the full spectrum of sports engagement is essential for cross-domain analysis connecting health, education, youth policy, and national identity.

variable_strategies.justification: Cultural socialization in childhood is inherently multi-dimensional, spanning parental discussion habits, museum visits, book gifting, independent reading, and household book availability, so no single item captures the full construct. The questions use broadly compatible ordinal scales (4- and 6-category frequency/presence items) that reflect a common latent dimension of culturally enriched home environments. PCA on the first component is preferred over a simple sum because the items have slightly different scale widths (K=4 vs K=6) and the first PC will weight each item by its actual covariance with the latent trait, reducing noise from scale heterogeneity before binning to 5 quantiles.

Reading engagement is multi-dimensional, covering current book ownership, newspaper/magazine consumption across geographic scopes, and self-assessed reading ease, so aggregation is necessary to capture the full construct. The item p17|DEP is excluded because it measures a specific barrier type among non-readers (a conditional, branching question with low effective coverage) rather than a continuous engagement dimension, making it incompatible for aggregation. PCA is preferred over a sum score because the remaining items span K=2 to K=7 with varying entropy, and the first principal component will extract the shared variance in reading engagement while down-weighting scale-width differences.

p54|DEP is the gold-standard single-item measure of cultural identity, directly capturing self-identified racial/ethnic category (indigenous, mestizo, white, other) with the highest entropy in the cluster (H=1.71), making it both content-valid and maximally informative. The other items are either highly skewed and low-entropy proxies (p48, p34, p35 on indigenous language ability with H≤0.40) or conditional sub-questions (p49_2a_1) that apply only to a subset of respondents, making aggregation inappropriate due to missing data structure. Since requires_aggregation=False and a single well-validated item exists, using p54|DEP alone avoids the distortion that would arise from combining a near-constant language item with a more variable ethnicity item.

Attitudes toward cultural openness are inherently multi-dimensional, spanning preferences for cultural plurality, perceptions of foreign influence, normative opinions on cultural policy, and support for cross-cultural education, so aggregation across the attitudinal items is warranted. p29_1|DEP is excluded because it is a free-first-mention item about cultural preservation priorities (K=9, largely nominal) that does not share a common ordinal direction with the other Likert-style opinion items, making it incompatible for summation. PCA on the first component is chosen because the remaining items have varying scale widths (K=4 to K=6) and the first PC will capture the dominant pro- vs. anti-openness attitudinal dimension while accommodating scale heterogeneity before binning to 5 quantiles.

Sports engagement and media consumption is best captured by combining active participation (p19|DEP), frequency of watching sports on TV (p21|DEP), and breadth of sports viewing scope (p21_1|DEP), which together span both behavioral dimensions of the construct. p19_2a_1|DEP is excluded because it is a near-degenerate nominal item (H=0.19, K=12 sport types) applicable only to active practitioners, and p20|DEP and p22_1|DEP are opinion/attitude items about sports motivation and purpose rather than behavioral engagement measures, making them conceptually misaligned with the engagement construct. PCA is preferred because the three retained items have different scale widths (K=4–6) and the first component will extract the shared engagement variance before binning to 5 quantiles.

construct_clusters.name: economic_wellbeing_perception
construct_clusters.description: Measures how individuals evaluate their own and the country's economic situation, including intergenerational comparisons of living standards. Captures both personal and societal economic satisfaction as well as perceived upward or downward mobility across generations.

construct_clusters.name: job_quality_and_labor_conditions
construct_clusters.description: Measures the objective and subjective quality of current employment, including contract type, working hours, overtime compensation, social security coverage, income relative to peers, and satisfaction with advancement opportunities. Captures the multidimensional nature of decent work.

construct_clusters.name: job_search_and_employment_precarity
construct_clusters.description: Measures the experience of unemployment and job insecurity, including active job-seeking behavior, perceived probability of finding or losing work, duration of unemployment, and willingness to accept unfavorable job conditions. Reflects labor market vulnerability and precariousness.

construct_clusters.name: labor_union_attitudes
construct_clusters.description: Measures respondents' normative and evaluative orientations toward labor unions, including perceived necessity and overall opinion of unions' role. Captures the latent dimension of collective labor representation legitimacy.

construct_clusters.name: human_capital_and_employment_preparation
construct_clusters.description: Measures the relationship between education, training, and labor market readiness, including highest education attained, perceived educational adequacy for one's job, participation in professional development, and perceived utility of that training. Captures the latent construct of human capital investment and its labor market relevance.

sources.citation: Diener, E., Ng, W., Harter, J., & Arora, R. (2010). Wealth and happiness across the world: Material prosperity predicts life evaluation, whereas psychosocial prosperity predicts positive feeling. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 99(1), 52–61.
sources.relevance: Establishes the distinction between material/economic satisfaction and subjective wellbeing, directly relevant to measuring personal and societal economic evaluation.
sources.key_finding: Income and material prosperity strongly predict life evaluation (cognitive judgments about one's life), supporting the validity of economic satisfaction as a distinct measurable construct.

sources.citation: Graham, C., & Pettinato, S. (2002). Frustrated achievers: Winners, losers and subjective well-being in new market economies. Journal of Development Studies, 38(4), 100–140.
sources.relevance: Examines how individuals in developing and transitional economies perceive their economic situation relative to expectations and intergenerational comparisons.
sources.key_finding: Individuals who are objectively better off may report lower subjective economic wellbeing when their expectations or reference points (including parental living standards) are not met.

sources.citation: Latinobarómetro. (2020). Informe Latinobarómetro 2020. Corporación Latinobarómetro, Santiago de Chile.
sources.relevance: Provides comparable survey methodology for measuring economic satisfaction at personal and national levels across Latin America, including Mexico.
sources.key_finding: Mexican respondents consistently distinguish between personal economic satisfaction and national economic evaluation, with the latter being significantly more negative.

sources.citation: Campos-Vazquez, R. M., & Monroy-Gómez-Franco, L. A. (2016). La relación entre crecimiento económico y pobreza en México. Investigación Económica, 75(298), 77–113.
sources.relevance: Analyzes the relationship between macroeconomic conditions and perceived economic wellbeing in Mexico, providing context for intergenerational mobility perceptions.
sources.key_finding: Despite periods of economic growth, perceived economic wellbeing among lower-income Mexicans has not improved proportionally, suggesting a disconnect between macro indicators and subjective evaluations.

sources.citation: Kelley, J., & Evans, M. D. R. (2017). Societal inequality and individual subjective well-being: Results from 68 societies and over 200,000 individuals, 1981–2008. Social Science Research, 62, 1–23.
sources.relevance: Examines how societal-level inequality shapes individual perceptions of economic wellbeing, relevant to the country-level vs. personal evaluation distinction in this construct.
sources.key_finding: National inequality moderates the relationship between personal income and subjective economic wellbeing, with greater inequality amplifying dissatisfaction among lower earners.

sources.citation: International Labour Organization. (2013). Decent work indicators: Guidelines for producers and users of statistical and legal framework indicators (2nd ed.). ILO, Geneva.
sources.relevance: Provides the foundational framework for measuring decent work, including contract type, social security, working hours, and compensation—directly mapping onto the construct's indicators.
sources.key_finding: Decent work is a multidimensional construct requiring measurement across employment security, social protection, working time, and fair income dimensions.

sources.citation: Weller, J. (2012). Crecimiento, empleo y distribución de ingresos en América Latina. CEPAL, Serie Macroeconomía del Desarrollo, No. 122.
sources.relevance: Analyzes the quality of employment in Latin America, including Mexico, with attention to informality, contract types, and social security coverage.
sources.key_finding: A large share of employment growth in Latin America has been in low-quality jobs characterized by lack of contracts, absence of social security, and limited advancement opportunities.

sources.citation: Muñoz de Bustillo, R., Fernández-Macías, E., Antón, J. I., & Esteve, F. (2011). Measuring more than money: The social economics of job quality. Edward Elgar Publishing.
sources.relevance: Develops a comprehensive job quality index incorporating wages, working time, employment stability, and intrinsic job characteristics, closely aligned with this construct's question set.
sources.key_finding: Job quality is best captured as a composite of objective conditions (contract, hours, pay) and subjective evaluations (satisfaction with advancement), and these dimensions are only moderately correlated.

sources.citation: Levy, S. (2008). Good intentions, bad outcomes: Social policy, informality, and economic growth in Mexico. Brookings Institution Press.
sources.relevance: Directly addresses the Mexican context of labor informality, social security fragmentation, and intermediary employment—key issues captured by this construct.
sources.key_finding: Mexico's dual social protection system creates perverse incentives that perpetuate informal employment, making social security coverage a critical indicator of job quality.

sources.citation: Standing, G. (2011). The precariat: The new dangerous class. Bloomsbury Academic.
sources.relevance: Defines and theorizes employment precarity as a multidimensional condition encompassing job insecurity, willingness to accept inferior conditions, and chronic vulnerability—directly aligned with this construct.
sources.key_finding: The precariat is characterized not only by unstable employment but by the subjective experience of insecurity and the behavioral willingness to accept degraded work conditions.

sources.citation: Kalleberg, A. L. (2009). Precarious work, insecure workers: Employment relations in transition. American Sociological Review, 74(1), 1–22.
sources.relevance: Provides a sociological framework for understanding precarious work that integrates perceived job loss probability, job search behavior, and acceptance of unfavorable conditions.
sources.key_finding: Perceived job insecurity has independent negative effects on wellbeing beyond actual unemployment, and willingness to accept inferior conditions is a key behavioral indicator of precarity.

sources.citation: Mora Salas, M., & de Oliveira, O. (2009). La degradación del empleo asalariado en los albores del siglo XXI: Costa Rica y México. Papeles de Población, 15(61), 195–231.
sources.relevance: Directly examines employment precarity in Mexico, including job insecurity perceptions, search duration, and willingness to accept substandard conditions.
sources.key_finding: Employment degradation in Mexico manifests through increasing proportions of workers experiencing insecurity and accepting jobs below their qualifications or expected income.

sources.citation: Addison, J. T., & Portugal, P. (2002). Job search methods and outcomes. Oxford Economic Papers, 54(3), 505–533.
sources.relevance: Analyzes the relationship between job search behavior, duration, and outcomes, providing methodological grounding for measuring search activity and expected duration.
sources.key_finding: Active job search methods and duration are significant predictors of re-employment quality, and reservation wages decline with search duration, reflecting increasing precarity.

sources.citation: Bensusán, G. (2006). Diseño legal y desempeño real: Instituciones laborales en América Latina. Miguel Ángel Porrúa / UAM.
sources.relevance: Analyzes the institutional framework of labor unions in Mexico and how public perceptions of unions are shaped by their historical corporatist role and perceived effectiveness.
sources.key_finding: Mexican labor unions' corporatist legacy significantly shapes public attitudes, with many citizens viewing unions as instruments of political control rather than genuine worker representation.

sources.citation: Fairbrother, P., & Yates, C. (Eds.). (2003). Trade unions in renewal: A comparative study. Routledge.
sources.relevance: Provides comparative framework for understanding attitudes toward unions including perceived necessity and legitimacy, applicable to the Mexican context.
sources.key_finding: Public attitudes toward unions depend on perceived union effectiveness and independence from political parties, with declining legitimacy where unions are seen as co-opted.

sources.citation: Zapata, F. (2005). Tiempos neoliberales en México. El Colegio de México.
sources.relevance: Examines the transformation of labor relations in Mexico under neoliberal reforms, including declining union membership and changing public attitudes toward collective representation.
sources.key_finding: Neoliberal reforms in Mexico eroded union power and public trust in unions, but did not eliminate the perceived necessity of collective worker representation among large segments of the population.

research_reviews.gap_justification: While there is literature on Mexican labor unions as institutions, there is relatively limited survey-based research specifically measuring Mexican citizens' normative attitudes toward unions (perceived necessity and evaluative opinions) as a latent construct. Most existing research focuses on union membership, density, or institutional analysis rather than public opinion. This construct fills an important gap by capturing the legitimacy of collective labor representation from the citizen's perspective, which is critical for understanding labor policy preferences and democratic participation in Mexico. Given Mexico's 2019 labor reform mandating democratic union elections, measuring these attitudes provides timely baseline data on how the public perceives the evolving role of unions.

sources.citation: Becker, G. S. (1964). Human capital: A theoretical and empirical analysis, with special reference to education. University of Chicago Press.
sources.relevance: Foundational theory of human capital that frames education and training as investments yielding labor market returns, directly underpinning this construct's conceptualization.
sources.key_finding: Investment in education and training increases worker productivity and earnings, establishing the theoretical basis for measuring educational attainment and training as determinants of labor market outcomes.

sources.citation: Hanushek, E. A., Schwerdt, G., Woessmann, L., & Zhang, L. (2017). General education, vocational education, and labor-market outcomes over the lifecycle. Journal of Human Resources, 52(1), 48–87.
sources.relevance: Examines the relationship between type of education, perceived job-education match, and labor market outcomes over the life course.
sources.key_finding: Vocational training provides initial labor market advantages but general education yields better long-term adaptability, highlighting the importance of measuring perceived education-job fit.

sources.citation: Flores-Crespo, P. (2007). Ethnicity, identity and educational achievement in Mexico. International Journal of Educational Development, 27(3), 331–339.
sources.relevance: Analyzes the relationship between educational attainment and labor market outcomes in Mexico, with attention to how education-job mismatches affect perceived returns to schooling.
sources.key_finding: In Mexico, educational attainment does not uniformly translate into better employment, and perceived education-job mismatch is widespread, particularly among indigenous and rural populations.

sources.citation: OECD. (2019). Getting skills right: Future-ready adult learning systems. OECD Publishing.
sources.relevance: Provides framework for measuring adult training participation and its perceived utility for labor market improvement, directly relevant to the training items in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Participation in adult training varies significantly by prior education level, and perceived utility of training is a strong predictor of continued human capital investment.

sources.citation: De Ibarrola, M. (2016). Los desafíos que enfrenta la formación de los jóvenes para el trabajo. In Jóvenes y trabajo en México (pp. 45–78). El Colegio de México.
sources.relevance: Directly addresses the Mexican context of youth education-to-work transitions, perceived adequacy of education for employment, and training needs.
sources.key_finding: A significant proportion of Mexican workers perceive their formal education as inadequate for their jobs, and participation in professional training remains low, particularly in the informal sector.

variable_strategies.justification: Economic wellbeing perception is inherently multi-dimensional, spanning macro-level satisfaction (p1), personal satisfaction (p2), intergenerational retrospective comparison (p3), and prospective comparison (p4); no single item captures the full construct. All four items use compatible ordinal scales (5–6 categories) anchored around satisfaction or comparative standing, making mean aggregation appropriate. Mean scoring preserves the ordinal spacing and the resulting composite can be binned into ≤5 ordered categories for the bridge model, reducing item-level measurement error while maximizing construct coverage.

Job quality is multi-dimensional but the seven available items mix binary, nominal-categorical, and ordinal scales, making direct summation inappropriate across all items. The three selected items—social security coverage (p43), relative income self-assessment (p45_1), and satisfaction with advancement opportunities (p46_1)—together capture the core quality dimensions (benefits, compensation, career prospects) and share an ordinal or quasi-ordinal structure with the highest entropies in the cluster (H=1.54, 1.62, 1.81). PCA on the first component extracts the dominant latent quality dimension while handling residual scale differences, and the resulting scores are binned into 5 quantile categories for the bridge model.

Employment precarity is best captured by combining perceived job-loss risk (p48, H=1.86), desire to change jobs (p49, H=1.30), and recent active job-search behavior (p56, H=1.42), which together span the attitudinal and behavioral dimensions of precarity. The remaining items either have very low entropy (p50 H=0.41, p58 H=0.67, p59_x H≈0.58–0.61) indicating limited discriminatory power, or are numeric with sparse distributions (p57). PCA on the first component accommodates the mixed ordinal/binary structure of the three selected items and extracts the dominant precarity signal, binned into 5 quantile categories for the bridge.

p23 (perceived necessity of labor unions, H=2.21) shares the highest entropy with p24 but has stronger face validity as a direct attitudinal measure of union necessity, which is the conceptual core of this construct. p24 is described as open-ended/ordinal and likely captures opinions about union characteristics rather than a clean attitudinal stance, making it less suitable as a single bridge variable. p22 (worker rights respect) is conceptually adjacent but taps a distinct perception; given the construct's moderate importance (3/5) and the high entropy of p23 alone, a single well-defined ordinal item is sufficient for the bridge without risking scale-incompatibility artifacts from aggregation.

Human capital is inherently multi-dimensional, requiring coverage of both objective attainment (p67, highest education completed, H=2.31) and job-specific adequacy perceptions (p65, appropriate education for job, H=2.79; p66, necessity of specialized training, H=2.25). These three items carry the highest entropy in the cluster and together capture the supply-side human capital stock alongside its perceived match to labor market demands. PCA on the first component handles the differing category counts (14, 7, 11) and extracts the dominant human capital dimension, with scores binned into 5 quantile categories; p64 (education at first job) and p71–p72 (training participation/utility, H≈0.55–0.56) are excluded due to temporal redundancy with p67 and near-floor entropy respectively.

construct_clusters.name: perceived_situation_of_children_and_youth
construct_clusters.description: Measures respondents' perception of whether the general situation (social, economic) of children, adolescents, and young people in Mexico has improved or deteriorated over time, capturing a broad evaluative orientation toward generational well-being.

construct_clusters.name: children_rights_recognition_and_respect
construct_clusters.description: Measures the degree to which respondents believe children's rights are known, respected, and upheld in Mexico, including awareness of specific rights and perceptions of how well institutions honor them.

construct_clusters.name: child_participation_and_voice
construct_clusters.description: Measures attitudes toward the extent to which children and adolescents should have a say in decisions affecting them — in family, school, and public life — capturing normative beliefs about youth agency and civic inclusion.

construct_clusters.name: child_vulnerability_and_violence_exposure
construct_clusters.description: Measures beliefs about children's exposure to violence, the contexts in which violence occurs, and the normative acceptance of physical punishment, reflecting attitudes toward child protection and safety.

construct_clusters.name: youth_educational_opportunity_and_labor_prospects
construct_clusters.description: Measures perceptions of whether today's children and young people have better or worse access to education and quality employment compared to previous generations, capturing intergenerational mobility beliefs.

sources.citation: UNICEF México. (2018). Los derechos de la infancia y la adolescencia en México. UNICEF.
sources.relevance: Provides a comprehensive assessment of the situation of children and adolescents in Mexico, including social and economic indicators that mirror the evaluative orientation captured by this construct.
sources.key_finding: Despite improvements in some indicators (e.g., school enrollment), persistent inequalities in health, nutrition, and violence exposure continue to shape the well-being of Mexican children and adolescents.

sources.citation: Torney-Purta, J., Lehmann, R., Oswald, H., & Schulz, W. (2001). Citizenship and education in twenty-eight countries: Civic knowledge and engagement at age fourteen. IEA.
sources.relevance: Establishes the importance of public perceptions of youth well-being as a driver of civic engagement and policy support, relevant to understanding how evaluative orientations toward generational well-being function in survey research.
sources.key_finding: Public perceptions of youth conditions are strongly linked to support for social policies and civic participation across diverse national contexts.

sources.citation: Campos-Vázquez, R. M., & Monroy-Gómez-Franco, L. A. (2016). La relación entre crecimiento económico y pobreza en México. Investigación Económica, 75(298), 77–113.
sources.relevance: Examines economic conditions affecting Mexican families and youth, providing context for understanding public perceptions of whether economic situations for younger generations have improved or deteriorated.
sources.key_finding: Economic growth in Mexico has not translated uniformly into poverty reduction, particularly for younger cohorts, which shapes public perceptions of intergenerational decline.

sources.citation: Ben-Arieh, A. (2008). The child indicators movement: Past, present, and future. Child Indicators Research, 1(1), 3–16.
sources.relevance: Foundational work on measuring child well-being from a subjective and societal perception standpoint, directly relevant to the construct's focus on perceived generational well-being.
sources.key_finding: Subjective indicators of child well-being, including public perceptions of children's situations, are essential complements to objective measures for informing policy.

sources.citation: Liebel, M. (2012). Children's rights from below: Cross-cultural perspectives. Palgrave Macmillan.
sources.relevance: Analyzes how children's rights are understood, recognized, and practiced across different cultural contexts, including Latin America, directly relevant to measuring public awareness and normative beliefs about children's rights.
sources.key_finding: Recognition of children's rights varies significantly by cultural context and socioeconomic status, with gaps between formal legal recognition and everyday practice being particularly pronounced in developing countries.

sources.citation: González-Contró, M. (2011). ¿Menores o niñas, niños y adolescentes? Reflexiones en el contexto del debate en América Latina. In M. Carbonell & P. Salazar (Eds.), La reforma constitucional de derechos humanos: Un nuevo paradigma. UNAM.
sources.relevance: Examines the legal and cultural framing of children's rights in Mexico and Latin America, directly informing the construct's focus on how children should be categorized and what rights they hold.
sources.key_finding: The shift from a 'minors' framework to a 'rights-bearing subjects' framework in Mexican law has not been fully internalized by the public, creating a gap between legal norms and social perceptions.

sources.citation: Pérez-Expósito, L. (2015). Civic engagement in Mexican youth: The role of rights awareness and institutional trust. Journal of Youth Studies, 18(9), 1140–1156.
sources.relevance: Investigates how awareness of rights among Mexican youth relates to civic engagement, providing empirical grounding for the link between rights recognition and broader democratic attitudes.
sources.key_finding: Higher awareness of rights among Mexican youth is positively associated with civic engagement, but institutional distrust moderates this relationship.

sources.citation: CNDH (Comisión Nacional de los Derechos Humanos). (2019). Informe especial sobre la situación de los derechos de niñas, niños y adolescentes en México. CNDH.
sources.relevance: Official report documenting the state of children's rights in Mexico, providing a benchmark for understanding public perceptions of rights respect captured by this construct.
sources.key_finding: Despite a robust legal framework (Ley General de los Derechos de Niñas, Niños y Adolescentes, 2014), implementation gaps persist, particularly in education, health, and protection from violence.

sources.citation: Hart, R. A. (1992). Children's participation: From tokenism to citizenship. UNICEF Innocenti Essays, No. 4. UNICEF International Child Development Centre.
sources.relevance: Seminal work establishing the theoretical framework for children's participation as a right and developmental necessity, directly underpinning the construct's focus on normative beliefs about youth agency.
sources.key_finding: Children's participation exists on a ladder from manipulation and tokenism to genuine shared decision-making, and meaningful participation requires structural support from adults and institutions.

sources.citation: Lansdown, G. (2010). The realisation of children's participation rights: Critical reflections. In B. Percy-Smith & N. Thomas (Eds.), A handbook of children and young people's participation (pp. 11–23). Routledge.
sources.relevance: Critically examines the implementation of Article 12 of the UNCRC (children's right to be heard), relevant to measuring attitudes toward children's voice in family and public life.
sources.key_finding: Cultural norms around adult authority remain the primary barrier to children's meaningful participation, even in countries that have ratified the UNCRC.

sources.citation: Corona Caraveo, Y., & Linares Pontón, M. E. (2007). Participación infantil y juvenil en el contexto mexicano. In Y. Corona Caraveo (Ed.), Diálogo de saberes sobre participación infantil. UAM-Xochimilco/UNICEF.
sources.relevance: Directly examines children's and adolescents' participation in the Mexican context, including cultural barriers and institutional opportunities, making it highly relevant to this construct.
sources.key_finding: Mexican children's participation is constrained by authoritarian family structures and limited institutional channels, but community-based programs have shown promise in fostering youth voice.

sources.citation: Tisdall, E. K. M. (2017). Conceptualising children and young people's participation: Examining vulnerability, social accountability and co-production. International Journal of Human Rights, 21(1), 59–75.
sources.relevance: Provides a contemporary theoretical framework linking children's participation to broader concepts of social accountability and co-production, relevant to the construct's civic inclusion dimension.
sources.key_finding: Children's participation is most effective when embedded in accountability structures rather than treated as a standalone right, requiring shifts in adult attitudes and institutional design.

sources.citation: Azaola, E. (2006). La violencia contra niños y adolescentes en México. Desacatos, (21), 7–10.
sources.relevance: Directly examines violence against children and adolescents in Mexico, including family, institutional, and community contexts, providing essential background for this construct.
sources.key_finding: Violence against children in Mexico is deeply embedded in cultural norms that normalize physical punishment and is exacerbated by poverty, marginalization, and weak institutional protections.

sources.citation: Straus, M. A. (2010). Prevalence, societal causes, and trends in corporal punishment by parents in world perspective. Law and Contemporary Problems, 73(2), 1–30.
sources.relevance: Provides a global comparative framework for understanding normative acceptance of physical punishment, directly relevant to the construct's inclusion of attitudes toward hitting children.
sources.key_finding: Corporal punishment prevalence varies widely across nations but is declining globally; cultural norms and legal frameworks are the strongest predictors of its acceptance.

sources.citation: Frías-Armenta, M., & McCloskey, L. A. (1998). Determinants of harsh parenting in Mexico. Journal of Abnormal Child Psychology, 26(2), 129–139.
sources.relevance: Empirically examines determinants of harsh parenting practices in Mexico, providing direct evidence on the cultural and socioeconomic factors that shape violence exposure beliefs.
sources.key_finding: Harsh parenting in Mexico is predicted by parents' own experiences of violence, poverty, and low education, suggesting intergenerational transmission of violence norms.

sources.citation: INEGI. (2015). Encuesta de Cohesión Social para la Prevención de la Violencia y la Delincuencia (ECOPRED). INEGI.
sources.relevance: National survey measuring violence exposure among Mexican youth across multiple contexts (home, school, community), providing a measurement parallel to the construct's focus.
sources.key_finding: A significant proportion of Mexican adolescents report exposure to violence in multiple settings, with home and school being the most common contexts.

sources.citation: Pinheiro, P. S. (2006). World report on violence against children. United Nations Secretary-General's Study on Violence against Children.
sources.relevance: Landmark UN report establishing the global framework for understanding children's vulnerability to violence across settings, directly relevant to the construct's multi-context approach.
sources.key_finding: Violence against children occurs in all settings—home, school, community, and institutions—and is both a human rights violation and a public health crisis requiring comprehensive prevention strategies.

sources.citation: Solís, P. (2018). Barreras estructurales a la movilidad social intergeneracional en México: Un enfoque multidimensional. In R. Vélez Grajales, J. E. Huerta Wong, & R. Campos Vázquez (Eds.), México, ¿tierra de oportunidades? Centro de Estudios Espinosa Yglesias.
sources.relevance: Directly examines intergenerational social mobility in Mexico across education and labor dimensions, providing the empirical foundation for understanding public perceptions of opportunity change.
sources.key_finding: Structural barriers—including educational quality gaps, labor market segmentation, and regional inequality—severely limit intergenerational mobility in Mexico, particularly for indigenous and rural populations.

sources.citation: De Hoyos, R., Rogers, H., & Székely, M. (2016). Ninis en América Latina: 20 millones de jóvenes en busca de oportunidades. World Bank Group.
sources.relevance: Examines the 'NiNi' (neither in education nor employment) phenomenon in Latin America, directly relevant to the construct's focus on youth labor and educational prospects.
sources.key_finding: Approximately 20 million young people in Latin America are neither in education nor employment, with Mexico having one of the highest rates, driven by low educational quality and labor market informality.

sources.citation: Torche, F. (2014). Intergenerational mobility and inequality: The Latin American case. Annual Review of Sociology, 40, 619–642.
sources.relevance: Provides a comparative framework for understanding intergenerational mobility beliefs in Latin America, relevant to the construct's focus on whether younger generations have better or worse prospects.
sources.key_finding: Latin America exhibits lower intergenerational mobility than other regions, with education serving as both a potential equalizer and a mechanism for reproducing inequality depending on quality and access.

sources.citation: Levy, S. (2008). Good intentions, bad outcomes: Social policy, informality, and economic growth in Mexico. Brookings Institution Press.
sources.relevance: Analyzes how Mexico's social policy and labor market structure affect economic opportunities for younger generations, providing context for public perceptions of declining job quality.
sources.key_finding: Mexico's dual labor market—with a large informal sector offering low wages and no benefits—undermines intergenerational economic mobility and shapes perceptions of declining opportunity.

sources.citation: OECD. (2019). Education at a Glance 2019: OECD Indicators. OECD Publishing.
sources.relevance: Provides comparative data on educational access and returns to education in Mexico, relevant to measuring perceptions of educational opportunity change across generations.
sources.key_finding: While educational attainment in Mexico has increased significantly across generations, returns to education have stagnated and quality gaps persist, potentially explaining perceptions of limited improvement.

variable_strategies.justification: All three questions use the same 6-category scale measuring perceived change since 2010, making them directly combinable via mean scoring. The construct is explicitly multi-dimensional—covering children, adolescents, and youth economic conditions—and no single item captures the full breadth of perceived situational change. Averaging reduces item-specific measurement error and yields a stable ordinal composite that can be binned into ≤5 categories for the bridge model.

The six questions in this cluster use incompatible scales and tap very different facets (knowledge of rights, normative opinions, specific right beliefs), making meaningful aggregation problematic. p22|NIN—asking how much children's rights are respected in Mexico—has the highest entropy (H=1.97) and is the most direct, face-valid measure of the construct's core evaluative dimension. Its 7-category response scale provides sufficient ordinal resolution for the bridge regression without conflating distinct sub-constructs.

p12, p13, and p48_1 all use 7-category Likert-type scales and together capture the attitudinal core of the construct: how much weight children's voices should carry in family and school decisions, and whether adolescent rights are seen as threatening adult authority. p16_1 is a first-mention categorical probe with a different response logic, and p41 has 12 categories covering a distinct behavioral context (public marches), so both are excluded to preserve scale compatibility. Averaging the three compatible items and binning to ≤5 categories yields a coherent, low-noise composite of adult attitudes toward child participation.

The four questions span fundamentally incompatible response formats—a 4-category belief item, an 8-category location probe, a 10-category open-ended justification item, and a 10-category family-problems item—making aggregation methodologically unsound. p26_1|NIN has the highest entropy (H=2.88) and directly taps the most sensitive and policy-relevant dimension of the construct: under what circumstances adults justify hitting a child, which is a strong proxy for normative tolerance of violence against children. Its 10 categories can be recoded into a meaningful ordinal scale (never justified → always justified) for the bridge model.

p28_1, p82, and p83_1 all use comparative ordinal scales (fewer/same/more or worse/same/better framing) that share a common directional logic of intergenerational opportunity comparison, making them conceptually and statistically combinable. p8|NIN uses a 0–10 ease-of-access scale with a different response format, and p81_2 taps family formation rather than education/labor, so both are excluded to maintain construct coherence. PCA on the three compatible items extracts the dominant latent dimension of perceived intergenerational opportunity change, and binning the first component into 5 quantiles produces a well-distributed ordinal variable suitable for the bridge regression.

construct_clusters.name: family_cohesion_quality
construct_clusters.description: Measures the perceived quality of interpersonal relationships and sense of unity within the family, capturing both co-resident and non-resident family bonds and the factors respondents believe sustain or fracture them.

construct_clusters.name: ideal_family_normativity
construct_clusters.description: Captures respondents' normative beliefs about what constitutes an ideal family structure, what prevents its achievement in Mexico, and what actions could bring it about — reflecting underlying traditionalism versus pluralism in family ideology.

construct_clusters.name: intergenerational_obligations
construct_clusters.description: Measures beliefs about reciprocal duties between parents and children, including how long children should remain in the parental home and who bears responsibility for elder care, reflecting attitudes toward familial solidarity across generations.

construct_clusters.name: conjugal_union_attitudes
construct_clusters.description: Measures attitudes and behaviors related to forming, maintaining, and dissolving conjugal unions, including motivations for partnering, tolerance of infidelity, and perceived causes of divorce — reflecting normative orientations toward marriage and partnership stability.

construct_clusters.name: family_value_transmission
construct_clusters.description: Captures the values respondents consider most important within the family, what they learned from their family of origin and practice daily, and what fundamental values parents should pass on to children — reflecting the perceived role of the family as a moral socialization institution.

sources.citation: Olson, D. H. (2000). Circumplex model of marital and family systems. Journal of Family Therapy, 22(2), 144–167.
sources.relevance: Foundational model defining family cohesion as emotional bonding among family members, directly informing the measurement of perceived unity and relationship quality within families.
sources.key_finding: Family cohesion, along with flexibility and communication, is a core dimension predicting family functioning, with balanced levels associated with healthier outcomes.

sources.citation: Villarreal, R., Blozis, S. A., & Widaman, K. F. (2005). Factorial invariance of a pan-Hispanic familism scale. Hispanic Journal of Behavioral Sciences, 27(4), 409–425.
sources.relevance: Examines familism — the strong identification and attachment to nuclear and extended family — among Hispanic populations, directly relevant to measuring family bond quality in Mexico.
sources.key_finding: A multidimensional familism scale demonstrated factorial invariance across Hispanic subgroups, confirming that family cohesion and obligation are culturally salient and reliably measurable constructs.

sources.citation: Esteinou, R. (2008). La familia nuclear en México: Lecturas de su modernidad, siglos XVI al XX. CIESAS.
sources.relevance: Provides historical and sociological analysis of the Mexican nuclear family, including the values and relational dynamics that sustain family unity in the Mexican context.
sources.key_finding: Mexican family cohesion has been shaped by a blend of Catholic moral tradition and modernization processes, with emotional bonds and shared values remaining central to family unity.

sources.citation: Rivera-Heredia, M. E., & Andrade-Palos, P. (2010). Escala de evaluación de las relaciones intrafamiliares (E.R.I.). Uaricha, Revista de Psicología, 14, 12–29.
sources.relevance: Develops and validates a Mexican-specific scale for assessing intrafamily relationships, including dimensions of union/support, expression, and difficulties.
sources.key_finding: The E.R.I. scale demonstrated strong psychometric properties in Mexican samples, confirming that family union-support, emotional expression, and conflict are distinct measurable dimensions of family relationship quality.

sources.citation: Ariza, M., & de Oliveira, O. (2001). Familias en transición y marcos conceptuales en redefinición. Papeles de Población, 7(28), 9–39.
sources.relevance: Analyzes the transformation of family structures in Latin America and the conceptual frameworks needed to understand normative ideals versus empirical family diversity in Mexico.
sources.key_finding: Mexican families are undergoing significant structural diversification, yet normative ideals of the nuclear, two-parent family persist as dominant aspirational models, creating tension between ideology and lived experience.

sources.citation: Quilodrán, J. (2003). La familia, referentes en transición. Papeles de Población, 9(37), 51–82.
sources.relevance: Examines how normative references about family have shifted in Mexico, including changing attitudes toward cohabitation, single parenthood, and non-traditional family forms.
sources.key_finding: Despite demographic transitions leading to greater family diversity, Mexican public opinion retains strong normative preferences for traditional family arrangements, though younger cohorts show increasing pluralism.

sources.citation: Robichaux, D. (2007). Familia y diversidad en América Latina: Estudios de casos. CLACSO.
sources.relevance: Provides comparative case studies of family diversity across Latin America, including Mexico, challenging the assumption of a single normative family model.
sources.key_finding: Latin American family systems exhibit significant regional and ethnic diversity that is often obscured by dominant normative discourses favoring the Western nuclear family ideal.

sources.citation: Esteinou, R. (2004). El surgimiento de la familia nuclear en México. Estudios de Historia Novohispana, 31, 99–136.
sources.relevance: Traces the historical emergence of the nuclear family ideal in Mexico, providing context for understanding contemporary normative beliefs about ideal family structure.
sources.key_finding: The nuclear family ideal in Mexico was consolidated through colonial-era Catholic doctrine and state modernization policies, becoming deeply embedded in cultural normativity.

causal_relationships.related_construct: attitudes toward human rights (e.g., same-sex families)

sources.citation: Sabogal, F., Marín, G., Otero-Sabogal, R., Marín, B. V., & Perez-Stable, E. J. (1987). Hispanic familism and acculturation: What changes and what doesn't? Hispanic Journal of Behavioral Sciences, 9(4), 397–412.
sources.relevance: Identifies familial obligations as a core dimension of Hispanic familism, including perceived duties of children toward aging parents and reciprocal expectations across generations.
sources.key_finding: Familial obligations — the belief that family members have a duty to provide material and emotional support — remain robust across acculturation levels among Hispanic populations.

sources.citation: Montes de Oca, V. (2004). Envejecimiento y protección familiar en México: Límites y potencialidades del apoyo al interior del hogar. In M. Ariza & O. de Oliveira (Eds.), Imágenes de la familia en el cambio de siglo. UNAM.
sources.relevance: Directly examines intergenerational support obligations in Mexico, focusing on elder care expectations and the limits of family-based social protection.
sources.key_finding: Mexican families remain the primary source of elder care, but urbanization, migration, and women's labor force participation are straining traditional intergenerational support systems.

sources.citation: Robles-Silva, L. (2007). La invisibilidad del cuidado a los enfermos crónicos: Un estudio cualitativo en el barrio de Oblatos. Editorial Universitaria.
sources.relevance: Qualitative study of caregiving obligations within Mexican families, revealing gendered and generational dynamics in who provides care and why.
sources.key_finding: Caregiving for chronically ill family members in Mexico falls disproportionately on women and is sustained by deeply held norms of filial obligation, often at significant personal cost.

sources.citation: Wong, R., & Higgins, M. (2007). Dynamics of intergenerational assistance in middle- and old-age in Mexico. In J. L. Angel & K. E. Whitfield (Eds.), The health of aging Hispanics (pp. 99–120). Springer.
sources.relevance: Quantitatively analyzes patterns of intergenerational transfers (financial, instrumental, emotional) in Mexico using longitudinal data.
sources.key_finding: Intergenerational assistance in Mexico flows bidirectionally but is shaped by household structure, proximity, and economic need, with older adults both giving and receiving support.

causal_relationships.related_construct: aging population and elder care needs

sources.citation: Ojeda, N. (2007). Matrimonio y unión libre en la percepción de adolescentes mexicanos radicados en Tijuana. Frontera Norte, 19(37), 41–68.
sources.relevance: Examines Mexican attitudes toward marriage versus cohabitation, including motivations for partnering and normative expectations around conjugal unions.
sources.key_finding: Mexican adolescents increasingly view cohabitation as a legitimate alternative to marriage, though marriage retains strong normative value, particularly among women and more religious respondents.

sources.citation: Amato, P. R. (2010). Research on divorce: Continuing trends and new developments. Journal of Marriage and Family, 72(3), 650–666.
sources.relevance: Comprehensive review of divorce research including attitudes toward divorce, causes of marital dissolution, and tolerance of infidelity — all directly measured in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Attitudes toward divorce have become more permissive over time, but economic stress, infidelity, and communication problems remain the most commonly cited causes of marital dissolution across cultures.

sources.citation: García, B., & Rojas, O. (2002). Los hogares latinoamericanos durante la segunda mitad del siglo XX: Una perspectiva sociodemográfica. Estudios Demográficos y Urbanos, 17(2), 261–288.
sources.relevance: Analyzes changes in household and conjugal union formation patterns in Latin America, including Mexico, providing demographic context for attitudinal shifts.
sources.key_finding: Latin American conjugal unions have diversified significantly, with rising cohabitation, delayed marriage, and increasing divorce rates reflecting broader socioeconomic and cultural transformations.

sources.citation: Hirsch, J. S. (2003). A courtship after marriage: Sexuality and love in Mexican transnational families. University of California Press.
sources.relevance: Ethnographic study of conjugal relationships in Mexican families, including attitudes toward fidelity, partnership expectations, and the impact of migration on marital dynamics.
sources.key_finding: Mexican conjugal relationships are shifting from models based on respect and obligation toward companionate ideals emphasizing emotional intimacy and mutual trust, though infidelity norms remain gendered.

causal_relationships.related_construct: human rights attitudes (e.g., divorce rights, domestic violence)

sources.citation: Schönpflug, U. (2001). Intergenerational transmission of values: The role of transmission belts. Journal of Cross-Cultural Psychology, 32(2), 174–185.
sources.relevance: Theoretical framework for understanding how values are transmitted from parents to children, directly relevant to the construct's focus on the family as a moral socialization institution.
sources.key_finding: Value transmission is most effective when parents are intentional, when the cultural context supports the values, and when parent-child relationship quality is high.

sources.citation: Rojas, O. L. (2016). Mujeres, hombres y vida familiar en México: Género y desigualdad. El Colegio de México.
sources.relevance: Examines the role of Mexican families in transmitting gendered values and moral orientations, including the influence of religion and institutional context.
sources.key_finding: Mexican families serve as primary agents of value socialization, but the values transmitted are increasingly contested as women's roles change and institutional influences (church, media, school) compete with family influence.

sources.citation: Schwartz, S. H. (2012). An overview of the Schwartz theory of basic values. Online Readings in Psychology and Culture, 2(1), 1–20.
sources.relevance: Provides the dominant theoretical framework for classifying human values (e.g., tradition, benevolence, conformity) that underlie family value transmission processes.
sources.key_finding: Values are organized in a universal circular structure where adjacent values are compatible and opposing values conflict, and family socialization is a key mechanism for value acquisition.

sources.citation: Alduncin, E. (2004). Los valores de los mexicanos. Banamex.
sources.relevance: Large-scale empirical study of Mexican values including family-related values, providing direct context for understanding what values Mexicans prioritize and transmit within families.
sources.key_finding: Family remains the most valued institution among Mexicans, with respect, honesty, and responsibility consistently ranked as the most important values to transmit to children.

sources.citation: Díaz-Guerrero, R. (2003). Bajo las garras de la cultura: Psicología del mexicano 2. Trillas.
sources.relevance: Classic work on Mexican cultural psychology examining how family-based sociocultural premises (e.g., obedience, respect, affiliation) shape personality and value orientations.
sources.key_finding: Mexican culture transmits a distinctive set of sociocultural premises through the family, including strong affiliative values and respect for authority, which serve as foundational scripts for social behavior.

variable_strategies.justification: Family cohesion quality is inherently multi-dimensional, requiring both internal (co-resident) and external (non-resident) relationship quality to be captured adequately. p25 and p26 are the most directly face-valid items measuring relational quality and use closely comparable ordinal response scales (K=8 and K=7), making them suitable for mean aggregation followed by binning. p27 and p52 tap into attributional beliefs about family dynamics rather than quality per se, and p53 measures values rather than cohesion, so they are excluded to preserve construct validity.

The core of ideal family normativity is the respondent's direct conception of what an ideal family looks like, which is precisely what p13 measures. While p14, p15, p67, and p68 address barriers, actions, and change perceptions, they are instrumentally or temporally framed rather than normative in the strict sense; p16 addresses definitional plurality rather than aspiration. p13 is the gold-standard single item for this construct, and its K=8 ordinal structure provides sufficient categorical resolution for the bridge model without requiring aggregation across conceptually heterogeneous items.

Intergenerational obligations span both downward (parental duties to children) and upward (filial duties to parents) directions, and the age-at-home-leaving norm (p30) operationalizes the practical boundary of these obligations; together these three items triangulate the construct's core normative content. PCA on the first component is preferred because the items have different numbers of categories (K=7, 5, 6) and likely tap correlated but not identical sub-dimensions, so the first principal component will extract the shared variance without assuming equal scale units. p31 is excluded as it is a follow-up reason item (H=0.83, low entropy, low variance), and p62–p64 shift toward personal preparedness and caregiving behavior rather than normative obligation beliefs.

The items in this cluster span conceptually distinct sub-domains—spouse selection criteria, family opinion salience, union initiation motives, infidelity attitudes, and divorce explanations—making aggregation across them construct-invalid rather than informative. p32 (reasons for extramarital affairs, H=2.46) has the highest entropy and captures a normatively charged, attitudinally rich dimension of conjugal union attitudes that reflects underlying value orientations about fidelity and partnership. Using the single highest-entropy item avoids conflating these heterogeneous attitudinal facets while still providing a meaningful ordinal variable for the bridge regression.

Family value transmission is best captured by combining the values learned and practiced by the respondent (p54), the values parents should transmit (p55), and the institutional locus of family influence (p56), as these three items jointly reflect both the content and the channel of intergenerational value transmission. All three use comparable ordinal categorical scales (K=6 each) with moderate-to-high entropy (1.79, 1.92, 2.03), and PCA will extract the shared normative orientation underlying them without forcing equal weighting. p57 (religion's influence) and p10 (family support vs. hindrance of goals) are excluded because they tap specific institutional or biographical dimensions that would dilute the core transmission construct.

construct_clusters.name: science_technology_self_efficacy
construct_clusters.description: Measures respondents' perceived ability to understand and engage with science and technology, including self-assessed knowledge levels and agreement that science is too specialized to comprehend. Captures the subjective sense of competence in scientific and technological domains.

construct_clusters.name: science_technology_interest_engagement
construct_clusters.description: Measures the degree to which respondents are actively interested in and seek out information about science and technology, including frequency of media consumption, expressed interest levels, and desire to participate in related activities.

construct_clusters.name: institutional_trust_in_science
construct_clusters.description: Measures respondents' trust in and perceived integrity of scientific institutions and actors, including skepticism about scientists' motivations, confidence in research disciplines, and perceived commitment of Mexican scientists to society.

construct_clusters.name: perceived_societal_value_of_science_technology
construct_clusters.description: Measures beliefs about the practical contribution and importance of science and technology to solving Mexico's social problems, including perceived usefulness of scientific knowledge, quality of science education, and expectations for future contributions.

construct_clusters.name: household_science_cultural_capital
construct_clusters.description: Measures the presence of knowledge-related resources in the household that reflect exposure to scientific and educational culture, including scientific, technical, encyclopedic, and history books, as well as access to digital infrastructure such as computers and internet.

sources.citation: Bauer, M. W., Allum, N., & Miller, S. (2007). What can we learn from 25 years of PUS survey research? Liberating and expanding the agenda. Public Understanding of Science, 16(1), 79–95.
sources.relevance: Foundational review of public understanding of science surveys, including self-assessed knowledge and perceived competence as key dimensions of scientific literacy.
sources.key_finding: Self-assessed scientific knowledge is a distinct dimension from factual knowledge and independently predicts attitudes toward science.

sources.citation: Bandura, A. (1997). Self-efficacy: The exercise of control. W.H. Freeman.
sources.relevance: Provides the theoretical foundation for self-efficacy as a construct, applicable to domain-specific competence beliefs including science comprehension.
sources.key_finding: Self-efficacy beliefs regulate motivation, affect, and action, and are domain-specific rather than global traits.

sources.citation: Ramos, C., & Peiró, J. M. (2012). Percepción pública de la ciencia en México: Análisis de la Encuesta Nacional de Ciencia y Tecnología. Revista Iberoamericana de Ciencia, Tecnología y Sociedad, 7(20), 1–25.
sources.relevance: Directly analyzes Mexican public opinion data on science, including self-assessed knowledge and perceived difficulty of understanding science.
sources.key_finding: Mexican respondents who perceive science as too specialized report lower engagement and more negative attitudes toward scientific institutions.

sources.citation: Eurobarometer. (2010). Science and Technology Report (Special Eurobarometer 340). European Commission.
sources.relevance: Large-scale survey instrument measuring self-assessed scientific knowledge and perceived ability to understand science, providing cross-national benchmarks.
sources.key_finding: Self-assessed knowledge of science correlates moderately with objective knowledge and strongly predicts interest in science news.
