sources.citation: Bauer, M. W., & Howard, S. (2012). The culture of science in modern Spain: An analysis of survey data. Public Understanding of Science, 21(3), 325–342.
sources.relevance: Examines interest in and engagement with science as a cultural variable, using survey items on media consumption and expressed interest similar to the proposed construct.
sources.key_finding: Interest in science is a multidimensional construct encompassing attentive, interested, and residual publics, each with distinct media consumption patterns.

sources.citation: Miller, J. D. (2004). Public understanding of, and attitudes toward, scientific research: What we know and what we need to know. Public Understanding of Science, 13(3), 273–294.
sources.relevance: Defines the 'attentive public' for science based on expressed interest and information-seeking behavior, directly relevant to this construct.
sources.key_finding: Only about 10-15% of the public qualifies as 'attentive' to science policy, but this group disproportionately influences public discourse.

sources.citation: CONACYT. (2017). Encuesta sobre la Percepción Pública de la Ciencia y la Tecnología en México (ENPECYT). Consejo Nacional de Ciencia y Tecnología.
sources.relevance: The Mexican national survey instrument from which many of these items likely derive, measuring interest, media consumption, and desire to participate in science activities.
sources.key_finding: Mexican respondents show moderate interest in science topics but low rates of active information-seeking through specialized media.

sources.citation: Nisbet, M. C., Scheufele, D. A., Shanahan, J., Moy, P., Brossard, D., & Lewenstein, B. V. (2002). Knowledge, reservations, or promise? A media effects model for public perceptions of science and technology. Communication Research, 29(5), 584–608.
sources.relevance: Models how media exposure shapes public engagement with science, directly relevant to the media consumption items in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Television and newspaper science coverage have differential effects on knowledge and attitudes, with print media more strongly associated with informed engagement.

sources.citation: Gauchat, G. (2012). Politicization of science in the public sphere: A study of public trust in the United States, 1974 to 2010. American Sociological Review, 77(2), 167–187.
sources.relevance: Examines long-term trends in public trust in science as an institution, showing how political ideology moderates trust—a framework applicable to the Mexican context.
sources.key_finding: Trust in science has become increasingly stratified by political orientation, with conservatives showing the steepest decline in institutional trust.

sources.citation: Dudo, A., & Besley, J. C. (2016). Scientists' prioritization of communication objectives for public engagement. PLoS ONE, 11(2), e0148867.
sources.relevance: Addresses public perceptions of scientists' motivations and integrity, directly relevant to items about skepticism toward scientists' commitments.
sources.key_finding: Scientists prioritize excitement and trust-building in public communication, but public perceptions of scientists' motivations remain mixed.

sources.citation: Huerta, E., & Korzenny, F. (2016). Confianza en las instituciones científicas en América Latina. Revista CTS, 11(33), 125–145.
sources.relevance: Analyzes trust in scientific institutions across Latin American countries including Mexico, using survey data comparable to the proposed construct.
sources.key_finding: Trust in scientific institutions in Latin America is moderate and strongly associated with perceived government investment in science and educational attainment.

sources.citation: Crettaz von Roten, F. (2009). European attitudes towards animal experimentation. Public Understanding of Science, 18(6), 686–698.
sources.relevance: Demonstrates how trust in scientists and research disciplines varies by domain and is shaped by perceived ethical commitments, relevant to discipline-specific confidence items.
sources.key_finding: Public trust in science varies significantly across research domains, with biomedical research receiving higher trust than social sciences.

sources.citation: Allum, N., Sturgis, P., Tabourazi, D., & Brunton-Smith, I. (2008). Science knowledge and attitudes across cultures: A meta-analysis. Public Understanding of Science, 17(1), 35–54.
sources.relevance: Meta-analysis showing the relationship between scientific knowledge and attitudes about the societal value of science across multiple countries, providing cross-cultural validation.
sources.key_finding: There is a modest positive correlation between scientific knowledge and positive attitudes toward science, but the relationship is moderated by cultural context.

sources.citation: Polino, C., & Castelfranchi, Y. (2012). The 'communicative turn' in contemporary techno-science: Latin American approaches and global tendencies. In B. Schiele et al. (Eds.), Science Communication in the World (pp. 3–17). Springer.
sources.relevance: Discusses Latin American perspectives on the social value of science, including expectations for science to address social problems—central to this construct.
sources.key_finding: Latin American publics tend to hold utilitarian views of science, valuing it primarily for its potential to solve pressing social and economic problems.

sources.citation: Vogt, C., & Polino, C. (Eds.). (2003). Percepción pública de la ciencia: Resultados de la encuesta en Argentina, Brasil, España y Uruguay. FAPESP/RICYT/OEI.
sources.relevance: Comparative Latin American survey study measuring perceived usefulness and societal contribution of science, directly analogous to this construct.
sources.key_finding: Across Latin American countries, majorities believe science and technology can improve quality of life, but significant minorities express concern about unequal access to benefits.

sources.citation: Dagnino, R. (2007). Ciencia y tecnología en América Latina: El modelo de la 'Triple Hélice' examinado desde el Sur. Redes, 13(26), 131–154.
sources.relevance: Critiques the application of science-society models in Latin America, relevant to understanding Mexican expectations about science solving national problems.
sources.key_finding: The disconnect between science policy and social needs in Latin America shapes public expectations and perceived value of scientific investment.

sources.citation: Bourdieu, P. (1986). The forms of capital. In J. Richardson (Ed.), Handbook of Theory and Research for the Sociology of Education (pp. 241–258). Greenwood.
sources.relevance: Foundational theoretical framework for cultural capital, including objectified cultural capital (books, resources) as indicators of household cultural environment.
sources.key_finding: Objectified cultural capital (e.g., books, instruments) functions as a transmitter of cultural dispositions and is strongly associated with educational reproduction.

sources.citation: Evans, M. D. R., Kelley, J., Sikora, J., & Treiman, D. J. (2010). Family scholarly culture and educational success: Books and schooling in 27 nations. Research in Social Stratification and Mobility, 28(2), 171–197.
sources.relevance: Demonstrates that household book ownership is a robust predictor of educational outcomes across diverse national contexts, validating books as cultural capital indicators.
sources.key_finding: Growing up in a household with 500 books propels a child 3.2 years further in education than a bookless home, regardless of parents' education or occupation.

sources.citation: Hargittai, E. (2002). Second-level digital divide: Differences in people's online skills. First Monday, 7(4).
sources.relevance: Extends cultural capital theory to digital resources, relevant to the computer and internet access items in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Access to digital infrastructure at home is a necessary but insufficient condition for digital literacy; it reflects and reinforces existing socioeconomic inequalities.

sources.citation: De Graaf, N. D., De Graaf, P. M., & Kraaykamp, G. (2000). Parental cultural capital and educational attainment in the Netherlands: A refinement of the cultural capital perspective. Sociology of Education, 73(2), 92–111.
sources.relevance: Empirically distinguishes between different forms of parental cultural capital and their effects on children's educational outcomes, relevant to the specific book types measured.
sources.key_finding: Reading climate in the home (measured partly through book types) has a stronger effect on educational attainment than participation in highbrow cultural activities.

variable_strategies.justification: Self-efficacy is inherently multi-dimensional, requiring multiple items to capture confidence across different science/technology contexts. However, the scales are incompatible (p16/p17 are 0–10 while p45_1/p37_1 are 7-point), making simple sum/mean inappropriate without standardization; PCA on the first component handles mixed-scale variance by working on standardized inputs and extracts the dominant latent dimension. All four items contribute meaningfully to the construct and PCA binned to 5 quantiles keeps the target within the ≤15 category constraint.

Interest and engagement in science/technology is a broad construct that no single item fully captures, and aggregation across multiple items reduces idiosyncratic measurement error. The six selected items all use 6-point scales (K=6), making them directly combinable via a mean score; p35 and p43 (K=5) are excluded to preserve scale homogeneity. The mean is then binned into ≤5 ordered categories, retaining ordinality while satisfying the bridge model's category constraint.

Institutional trust in science is a high-importance construct (5/5) that spans trust in scientific institutions, scientists, and regulatory bodies, requiring multiple items for adequate coverage. The scales are slightly mixed (p32_1, p33, p50_1 are K=6; p40_1, p49_1 are K=7), so PCA on standardized inputs is preferred over a raw mean to avoid scale-range bias. The first principal component captures the dominant shared trust dimension across all five items, and binning to 5 quantiles produces a well-distributed ordinal target.

Perceived societal value of science/technology is a high-importance, multi-faceted construct encompassing beliefs about economic, health, environmental, and social benefits, so aggregation is essential. The full item pool mixes K=6, K=7, and K=11 scales (p42_1, p39_1 excluded due to scale incompatibility or lower face validity for the societal-value dimension), while the six retained items split between K=6 (p34, p36, p44_1, p57, p58) and K=7 (p51_1); PCA on standardized scores handles this residual scale heterogeneity and extracts the dominant value-perception factor. Binning the first PC to 5 quantiles yields a compact ordinal variable suitable for the bridge regression.

Household science cultural capital is a latent stock variable best measured by summing indicators of science-related resources, activities, and habits present in the home, analogous to standard cultural capital indices. All seven items share an identical 4-point scale (K=4), making a simple sum score both methodologically sound and interpretable as a cumulative resource count. The summed score ranges from 7 to 28 and is binned into 5 ordered categories, capturing meaningful variation while remaining within the bridge model's category limit.

construct_clusters.name: perceived_education_quality
construct_clusters.description: Respondent's overall evaluation of the quality of education in Mexico, including perceived trends over time, a numeric rating, and belief about whether school content is relevant to real life and work.

construct_clusters.name: educational_returns_belief
construct_clusters.description: Respondent's beliefs about the instrumental and social value of education, including whether studying improves employment prospects, income, and societal well-being, and whether it is generally worthwhile.

construct_clusters.name: school_environment_quality
construct_clusters.description: Respondent's assessment of the physical and social conditions of their current school, including facility condition, safety perception, adequacy of laboratories, and teacher engagement with students.

construct_clusters.name: educational_inclusion_equity
construct_clusters.description: Respondent's perceptions of fairness and access in the educational system, including whether school is an option for all, whether there is capacity for all students, unfavorable treatment of women, and barriers faced by students with disabilities.

construct_clusters.name: digital_and_cultural_capital
construct_clusters.description: Respondent's access to and use of information and communication technologies, reading habits, and cultural participation, reflecting the stock of resources available for informal learning and social engagement.

sources.citation: Martínez-Rizo, F. (2012). Procedimientos para el estudio sobre prácticas docentes. Revista Electrónica de Investigación Educativa, 14(2), 1–21.
sources.relevance: Discusses frameworks for evaluating education quality in Mexico, including public perceptions of teaching practices and their alignment with real-life skills.
sources.key_finding: Public perceptions of education quality in Mexico are shaped by both observable teaching practices and broader systemic factors such as curriculum relevance.

sources.citation: Hanushek, E. A., & Woessmann, L. (2012). Do better schools lead to more growth? Cognitive skills, economic outcomes, and causation. Journal of Economic Growth, 17(4), 267–321.
sources.relevance: Establishes the link between perceived and measured education quality and economic outcomes, providing a theoretical basis for why subjective quality ratings matter.
sources.key_finding: Cognitive skill levels, which proxy education quality, are strongly associated with long-run economic growth, validating the importance of quality perceptions.

sources.citation: Andere, E. (2013). La escuela rota: Sistema y política en contra del aprendizaje en México. Siglo XXI Editores.
sources.relevance: Provides a comprehensive critique of the Mexican education system, arguing that structural deficiencies undermine quality and public trust.
sources.key_finding: Mexico's education system suffers from systemic misalignment between policy goals and classroom outcomes, contributing to declining public perceptions of quality.

sources.citation: OECD. (2019). Education at a Glance 2019: OECD Indicators. OECD Publishing.
sources.relevance: Offers comparative data on education quality indicators across OECD countries including Mexico, contextualizing subjective perceptions with objective metrics.
sources.key_finding: Mexico consistently ranks below the OECD average on quality indicators such as PISA scores, which aligns with public perceptions of stagnating or declining quality.

causal_relationships.related_construct: government education spending and policy reforms

sources.citation: Psacharopoulos, G., & Patrinos, H. A. (2018). Returns to investment in education: A decennial review of the global literature. Education Economics, 26(5), 445–458.
sources.relevance: Provides the foundational framework for understanding beliefs about returns to education, documenting global patterns in private and social returns.
sources.key_finding: Returns to education remain positive globally, with higher returns in developing countries, supporting the rationality of beliefs that education improves income and employment.

sources.citation: Levy, S. (2008). Good Intentions, Bad Outcomes: Social Policy, Informality, and Economic Growth in Mexico. Brookings Institution Press.
sources.relevance: Analyzes the disconnect between educational attainment and labor market outcomes in Mexico, particularly in the informal sector.
sources.key_finding: In Mexico, the expected returns to education are undermined by high informality, which may erode public beliefs about the instrumental value of schooling.

sources.citation: Carnoy, M. (2011). As higher education expands, is it contributing to greater inequality? National Institute Economic Review, 215(1), R34–R47.
sources.relevance: Examines whether expanding education systems deliver on promises of social mobility and reduced inequality, directly relevant to beliefs about societal benefits of education.
sources.key_finding: Educational expansion does not automatically reduce inequality; returns depend on labor market structures and quality of education, which shapes public beliefs about education's worth.

sources.citation: Mayer, S. E., & Lopoo, L. M. (2008). Government spending and intergenerational mobility. Journal of Public Economics, 92(1–2), 139–158.
sources.relevance: Connects beliefs about education's value to broader social mobility outcomes, relevant to the construct's societal dimension.
sources.key_finding: Public investment in education is associated with greater intergenerational mobility, providing an empirical basis for beliefs that education contributes to a better society.

sources.citation: INEE. (2018). La educación obligatoria en México: Informe 2018. Instituto Nacional para la Evaluación de la Educación.
sources.relevance: Documents the state of school infrastructure, safety, and teaching conditions across Mexico, directly measuring the components of this construct.
sources.key_finding: Significant disparities exist in school infrastructure quality across Mexican states, with rural and indigenous schools facing the worst conditions.

sources.citation: Uline, C., & Tschannen-Moran, M. (2008). The walls speak: The interplay of quality facilities, school climate, and student achievement. Journal of Educational Administration, 46(1), 55–73.
sources.relevance: Establishes the theoretical and empirical link between physical school environment, school climate, and student outcomes.
sources.key_finding: School facility quality is significantly associated with both school climate perceptions and student achievement, supporting the integration of physical and social environment measures.

sources.citation: Blanco, E. (2011). Los límites de la escuela: Educación, desigualdad y aprendizajes en México. El Colegio de México.
sources.relevance: Analyzes how school-level factors including infrastructure and teacher quality contribute to educational inequality in Mexico.
sources.key_finding: School environment quality, including teacher engagement and facility conditions, explains a significant portion of achievement gaps between socioeconomic groups in Mexico.

sources.citation: Pianta, R. C., Hamre, B. K., & Allen, J. P. (2012). Teacher-student relationships and engagement: Conceptualizing, measuring, and improving the capacity of classroom interactions. In S. L. Christenson et al. (Eds.), Handbook of Research on Student Engagement (pp. 365–386). Springer.
sources.relevance: Provides the conceptual framework for measuring teacher engagement and attention to students, which are key items in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Teacher-student interaction quality is one of the strongest school-level predictors of student engagement and learning outcomes.

sources.citation: Tomasevski, K. (2003). Education Denied: Costs and Remedies. Zed Books.
sources.relevance: Provides a rights-based framework for understanding educational inclusion, covering access, availability, acceptability, and adaptability—all reflected in this construct's items.
sources.key_finding: Educational exclusion is multidimensional, encompassing not only physical access but also capacity constraints, gender discrimination, and disability barriers.

sources.citation: Parker, S. W., & Pederzini, C. (2000). Gender differences in education in Mexico. In E. Katz & M. Correia (Eds.), The Economics of Gender in Mexico (pp. 9–45). World Bank.
sources.relevance: Directly examines gender-based inequities in Mexican education, relevant to the item on unfavorable treatment of women.
sources.key_finding: While gender gaps in enrollment have narrowed in Mexico, qualitative differences in treatment and expectations persist, particularly in rural areas.

sources.citation: Ainscow, M., & Miles, S. (2008). Making Education for All inclusive: Where next? Prospects, 38(1), 15–34.
sources.relevance: Provides the conceptual grounding for inclusive education, particularly for students with disabilities, directly relevant to the disability barriers item.
sources.key_finding: Inclusive education requires systemic changes beyond physical access, including attitudinal shifts and pedagogical adaptations.

sources.citation: Solís, P. (2013). Desigualdad social y efectos institucionales en las transiciones educativas. In Desigualdad en las trayectorias educativas y laborales (pp. 47–84). El Colegio de México.
sources.relevance: Analyzes how institutional capacity and social inequality interact to shape educational access and equity in Mexico.
sources.key_finding: Institutional capacity constraints disproportionately affect lower-income students in Mexico, reinforcing social stratification through the education system.

sources.citation: Bourdieu, P. (1986). The forms of capital. In J. Richardson (Ed.), Handbook of Theory and Research for the Sociology of Education (pp. 241–258). Greenwood.
sources.relevance: Provides the foundational theoretical framework for cultural capital, which this construct operationalizes through reading habits, museum attendance, and technology access.
sources.key_finding: Cultural capital, including embodied practices like reading and institutional engagement, is a key mechanism through which social inequality is reproduced across generations.

sources.citation: DiMaggio, P., Hargittai, E., Celeste, C., & Shafer, S. (2004). Digital inequality: From unequal access to differentiated use. In K. Neckerman (Ed.), Social Inequality (pp. 355–400). Russell Sage Foundation.
sources.relevance: Establishes the concept of digital inequality beyond mere access, encompassing use patterns for information and education—directly measured in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Digital inequality operates on multiple levels: access, skills, and purpose of use, with educational and informational uses conferring greater advantages.

sources.citation: Warschauer, M., & Matuchniak, T. (2010). New technology and digital worlds: Analyzing evidence of equity in access, use, and outcomes. Review of Research in Education, 34(1), 179–225.
sources.relevance: Reviews evidence on how technology access and use relate to educational outcomes, relevant to the ICT items in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Access to technology alone does not close educational gaps; the quality and purpose of technology use are critical mediators of educational benefit.

sources.citation: INEGI. (2020). Encuesta Nacional sobre Disponibilidad y Uso de Tecnologías de la Información en los Hogares (ENDUTIH). Instituto Nacional de Estadística y Geografía.
sources.relevance: Provides the Mexican context for digital access and use patterns, directly relevant to the ICT items in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Significant digital divides persist in Mexico along socioeconomic, geographic, and age lines, with internet and computer access remaining limited in rural and low-income households.

sources.citation: Savage, M., Devine, F., Cunningham, N., Taylor, M., Li, Y., Hjellbrekke, J., Le Roux, B., Friedman, S., & Miles, A. (2013). A new model of social class? Findings from the BBC's Great British Class Survey experiment. Sociology, 47(2), 219–250.
sources.relevance: Demonstrates how cultural capital (including cultural participation and media consumption) combines with other forms of capital to define social position, validating the composite nature of this construct.
sources.key_finding: Cultural engagement patterns, including reading and museum attendance, are strong markers of social class position and are associated with distinct life outcomes.

variable_strategies.justification: Perceived education quality is inherently multi-dimensional, spanning retrospective change assessment, absolute quality rating, and pedagogical relevance, so no single item captures the full construct. PCA is preferred over sum/mean because the items use incompatible scales (0–10 for p52, 5-point ordinal for p49 and p51), and the first principal component will extract the shared variance without imposing arbitrary equal weighting across mismatched metrics. p20 is excluded because its very low entropy (H=0.92) indicates a highly skewed, low-information distribution that would add noise rather than signal to the composite.

The three p55 items share an identical 4-point agree/disagree scale and each taps a distinct but theoretically coherent facet of returns belief (employment, income, societal contribution), making them directly summable into a composite score that is then binned to ≤5 categories. p37 is excluded because its 6-point scale is incompatible with the 4-point items and its very low entropy (H=0.91) suggests near-ceiling agreement that would distort the composite. Summing the three compatible items reduces individual-item measurement error while preserving the multi-faceted nature of returns beliefs.

The six items in this construct use highly incompatible scales (5-point, 7-point, 8-point, 9-point, and 14-point), making meaningful aggregation methodologically problematic without extensive rescaling that would introduce arbitrary assumptions. p30_1 has by far the highest entropy (H=2.36), indicating it provides the most discriminating information across respondents, and teacher instructional engagement is arguably the most central driver of perceived school environment quality. Given the importance rating of only 3/5 for this construct, the pragmatic single-item approach is justified over a complex multi-scale aggregation.

Educational inclusion and equity is a high-priority construct (5/5) that spans access universality, system capacity, gender equity, and disability access—dimensions that are conceptually related but not redundant, warranting aggregation. Although all items use 4- or 5-point scales that are broadly compatible, the varying number of categories and the distinct substantive angles (structural access vs. discriminatory treatment) make PCA preferable to a raw sum, as it weights items by their shared variance rather than treating all dimensions as equally informative. The first principal component will capture the latent 'equity orientation' dimension common to all four items while being robust to the slight scale heterogeneity.

Digital and cultural capital is a genuinely multi-dimensional construct blending technology access, digital use behaviors, and cultural consumption habits, so no single item is sufficient. PCA is chosen because the items mix binary-style access indicators (p47 series, 4-point) with frequency scales (p45 series, 7-point), and the first component will extract the shared socioeconomic capital gradient without imposing equal weights across these substantively and metrically distinct item types. p47_6 (mobile phone access, H=0.79) is excluded as its very low entropy and near-universal mobile penetration in contemporary surveys would contribute minimal discriminating variance and could suppress the first component's alignment with the capital construct.
